text: AI Applications in Medical Reporting and Diagnosis Manal Makram Department of Data Science FGSSR, Cairo University Cairo, Egypt manal.hana64gmail.com Ammar Mohammed 1,2 1Dept. of CS, FGSSR, Cairo University 2School of Computing and Digital Tech ESLSCA University, Cairo, Egypt AbstractThe integration of artificial intel- ligence (AI) in healthcare is revolutionizing di- agnosis and patient care by improving clinical documentation and the management of electronic health records that depend on medical image interpretation, increasing accuracy, and reducing time. Egypt ranks first in liver disease and sec- ond in liver cancer mortality worldwide in 2020. Large language models, a subset of AI techniques, can assist in disease diagnosis. LLM models with multimodal capabilities can classify and describe patient scan images and extract information from clinical notes. These models can extract vital di- agnoses with the support of prompt engineering, as one of these models can answer questions, summarize information, and translate complex medical terminology into plain language, enabling patients to understand their medical reports and diagnoses. There are two primary approaches to achieving this. First, fine-tuning can adapt the model to medical data, which can be resource- intensive. The second approach, pre-trained LLM models can be utilized to leverage pre-trained modelstoperformthenecessarytasks,focusingon effectively using prompts to guide the model for preciseandrelevantoutputs.Thisstudyhighlights the role of generative AI models by focusing on prompt engineering, and how carefully crafting prompts can enhance the effectiveness of LLM modelsinmedicalapplicationswithhighaccuracy. It demonstrates this through experiments using pre-trained models based on semantic similarity with GPT-4o and BioGPT. Implementing a zero- shot model for liver tumor classification is one of the prompt engineering techniques. The per- formance metrics achieved were impressive, accu- racy,precision,recall,andF1-scoresare88,81,88, and 83 percent, respectively. Index Terms Generative AI, Prompt Engi- neering, Large Language Model (LLM), Chat- GPT, Artificial Intelligence, Medical radiology reports I. Introduction Generative AI can be implemented

in prompt engineering as a cutting-edge approach in natural language processing (NLP) tasks by improving LLM performance 1, 2. The healthcare sector has re- cently received increasing attention due to its close relationship with each individual. In generative AI models, prompt engineering is user-provided textual input that directs the models output, simple queries to a complex problem. Prompt engineering is a computer program that can simulate conversations with human users. The generative AI applications in healthcare and medicine will grow in significance as they develop and become more precisely adapted to the particular conditions and demands of the medical domain. This is especially helpful when training data is lacking or certain jobs are too specialized for broad models, such as 36. Factors influencing prompt criteria are user intent, model understanding, domain specificity, clarity, specificity, and constraints 7. In artificial intelligence (AI), natural language processing (NLP) is a subfield that can transform unstructured clinical notes into structured data. It can also be used to identify medical conditions, med- ications, and names of lab tests through tasks like named entity recognition (NER). GPT models are a notable innovation in NLP that was first introduced by OpenAI 3. Large transformers, known as LLMs are com- posed of either an encoder, a decoder, or both and are trained in unsupervised learning on a large corpus of text. One of the earliest examples of this type of transformer is BERT, an encoder transformer trained in unsupervised learning to predict masked tokens. Next-token prediction was utilized by decoder-style transformers such as GPT. These models have sig- nificantly advanced various downstream tasks, in- 979-8-3503-6777-52431.00 2024 IEEE 185 2024 International Mobile, Intelligent, and Ubiquitous Computing Conference (MIUCC) 979-8-3503-6777-52431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109MIUCC62295.2024.10783552 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. cluding question answering, code completion, and summarizing. Several improvements in this model led to models like GPT-3, GPT-3.5, and most recently GPT-4 (OpenAI 2023) 8. Prompt engineering has demonstrated significant superiority across multiple areas and has gained major importance in healthcare because of recent improvements in LLM models. LLMs can connect radiation oncology data to clinical applications. Currently, oncologists often have to spend considerable time reviewing extensive clinical notes, which include a variety of text documents such as demographics, operation notes, pathology and radiology reports, lab results, discharge, and consult notes stored in the Electronic Medical Record (EMR) systems 9. LLM models like ChatGPT can continue evolving and improving medical applications, such as radiology reports, adding value and contributing to human health. Despite this, LLM models have many limitations, including transient state, proba- bilistic nature, outdated information, content fabri- cation, resource intensity, and domain specificity. So, prompt engineering techniques enhance LLM utility and mitigate inherent constraints. Prompt engineer- ing has more powerful tools like a chain of thought prompting, encouraging the model to be factual through other means, explicitly ending the prompt instructions, using the AI to correct itself, generat- ing different opinions, teaching an algorithm in the prompt, and affordances 10. Prompt engineering optimizes the interaction with the chatbot for exact findings. Prompt engineering is divided into manual prompts (zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot, embedding- based retrieval mechanisms) and automated prompts (discrete, continuous). Manual prompts depend on human-crafted prompts. A prompt that combines instructions, questions, input data, and examples 1, 7, 10. Automated prompts are generated using algorithms or automating methods that can save time and cost which collect task-specific information. Prompt engineering has many applications, including classification, generation, detection, augmentation, question answering, inference, Zero-shot learning, and embedding-based retrieval mechanisms. Prompt engineering can help simplify radiological reports,

extract information from oncology notes, and classify fractures using free-text reports even with a limited dataset 5, 1113. Also, image segmentation forms the basis of various clinical tasks, including surgical planning, diagnostic assistance, and surgical naviga- tion. Segmentation can be classified as Atlas-based, learning-based methods, and vision foundation mod- els 14. This paper delves into the development and methodology of innovative AI tools, aiming to address the challenges posed by explicit content in radiology reports. The integration of GPT-4, a state- of-the-art generative language model, plays a pivotal role in categorizing liver tumors. Section 2 demon- strates related work highlighting the effectiveness of prompt engineering across various tasks, showcasing its potential to enhance large language model (LLM) performance in medical contexts by building on AI techniques. Next, section 3 shows the methodology of prompt engineering for this work. After that, section 4 shows the experimental results and evaluation of the proposed prompt engineering. Finally, section 5 provides a conclusion and future work of the paper. II. Related Work Deep learning have been applied in varieties of application domains 1518. Among those domain, Medical doamin 1922. Particularly, several re- search efforts used LLM for medical domains. For example, the authors in 23 discussed Llama 3. The model is a dense transformer with 405B parameters and a context window of up to 128K tokens. Llama 3 integrates image, video, and speech capabilities with a compositional approach. Another research study 12 used clinical radiology cases to concate- nate prompting strategies with LLM ChatGPT and GPT4. Also, this paper 24 used GPT-4, GPT- 3.5-turbo, Gemma-7B and Llama3-8B. The authors employed prompt engineering to boost accuracy. GPT-4 achieved the highest accuracy in detecting disease status. The work in 11 explains that the study introduces a prompt construction module for medical language templates that enables extract

of contextual information from electronic health patient records and generates complete cell embedding. The authors in 25 demonstrated that AI models can accurately segment liver tumors without manual an- notation by using synthetic tumors in CT scans. The work in 14 concluded with the following contribu- tions. Firstly, the introduction of the SAM prompt engineering framework. Secondly, the development of two efficient image registration strategies that align images or prompts with the referenced dataset from the Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital. Third, the demonstration of improved performance compared to alternative approaches which require full segmenta- tion of the reference set and using SAM without the proposed registration strategy, for clinical image seg- mentation. So, the prompt engineering approach for segmentation foundation models depends on match- ing the input image or prompt to a small dataset of weakly labeled samples. This alignment allows the construction of effective prompts for segmentation foundation models to obtain accurate segmentation, even for out-of-distribution samples, using as few 186 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111000 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. as five-point prompt-labeled reference images. The experiments for the BERT and BART-based mod- els in work in 13 were conducted by comparing the performance of BERT-based models and prompt engineering on LLM. Every model was tuned for seven epochs, starting at a learning rate of 2e-05, 128 was the batch size, and 0.01 was the weight decay parameter. The Command-R and LLAMA3 models showed significantly better performance in their results. Authors in 26, hypothesize that ap- plying in-context learning from text-based models to medical images could be immensely beneficial. This approach is particularly relevant for underrep- resented medical cases, such as rare tumor types, which often receive insufficient representation in tra- ditional deep-learning training pipelines. To explore this,

they conducted experiments using the GPT- 4V model through the official OpenAI Python APIs chat completions endpoint, testing the model across various datasets. The reported performance metrics include an accuracy score of 82.7 for GPT-4V compared to 83.3 on the MHIST dataset and 92.7 compared to 88.3 on the PatchCamelyon dataset. These experiments used a zero-shot baseline as a comparison to evaluate the benefits of in-context learning. The authors have laid the groundwork for a general-purpose framework that advances state-of- the-art prompting techniques for image analysis. III. Methodology This paper employs prompt engineering archi- tectures for dealing with medical reports. The pro- posed method utilized the httpswww.eurorad.org dataset for experiments and evaluation. The dataset comprises a sample of multi-modality radiology re- ports that contain text and CT or MRI scans. The datasetwasdesignedtorepresentasampleofmedical reports containing clinical findings, associated clini- cal notes, and diagnostic details. Prompt engineer- ing can solve The complex and diverse professional terminology in the medical field, the wide range of professional knowledge, and ethical issues related to patient privacy in the healthcare field 11. In this paper, we fine-tuned BioBERT for medical reports using the GPT-4 model. Cosine similarities are calcu- lated with encoded dialogues from the training data to summarize each dialogue, where k3 with GPT- 4 along with section headers to get a summary for the given part. The value of k fits well with the context length of the prompting-based pipeline 27, 28. Accuracy was calculated as the proportion of correct predictions to the total predicted numbers in Eq.1. The accuracy steadily improves over the training epochs, reflecting the models increasing abilitytocorrectinterpret,andclassifymedicaltexts. Precision and recall metrics revealed the balance between false positives and false negatives, crucial for applications in clinical decision-making where the cost of errors varies significantly Eq.2, 3. The F1- score 29 highlighted

the overall effectiveness of the models, considering both precision and recall and showed consistent improvement, suggesting that the models were well-tuned to handle the complexities of the medical datasets Eq.4. Problem Identification and Motivation Recognizing the need for radiology-friendly ver- sions of medical radiology reports, our research ad- dresses the challenge of categorizing liver tumors in patient reports accurately. B. Objectives of the Design Develop an innovative tool that utilizes ad- vanced AI techniques to detect whether the patient is normal or abnormal systematically and the type of tumor if present. C. Design and Development We use the pre-trained model to perform the desiredtask.Promptengineeringcanbebrokendown into five key steps. First, we use an appropriate pre- trained model such as GPT-4o and BioGPT, suitable for generating radiology reports. Next, design the prompts for downstream tasks, which can be tailored to generate radiology reports this step is called the prompt engineering process. The third step involves designing responses based on the task at hand, al- lowing the model to produce the desired output. In the fourth step, we expand the paradigm to improve results or adaptability methods. Finally, researchers must design training strategies that enable the model to learn efficiently and effectively, save time, and improve accuracy in classifying medical texts or an- swering questions related to specific cases. Fig. 1 explains the following steps 1) Medical Reports Loadingload the radiology reports into memory using a Python library such as Pandas or Pydicom, depending on the format of the reports. That establishes a starting point for the analysis process by importing the

raw report data. 187 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111000 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 2) Text preprocessingutilize natural language processing (NLP) techniques to clean and standard- ize the text data from the reports that define key- words related to liver conditions such as liver, hep- atic, tumor, lesion, abscess, carcinoma, metastasis, cirrhosis, and foreign object. Then prepare the text for further analysis by removing noise, such as spe- cific unwanted patterns (like dates, DOIs, ISSNs, author names, etc.). Standardize formatting, and handling common abbreviations, is done by splitting the cleaned text into smaller, relevant sentences. 3) Named Entity Recognition (NER) apply a medical (NER) model, such as one based on BERT or BioBERT to identify and extract key medical entities from the report text to isolate important medical terms, anatomical structures, and findings for focused analysis. 4) Information Extraction that develop rule- based or machine-learning algorithms to extract spe- cific information such as diagnoses, measurements, and recommendations from the identified entities and surrounding context. The result is to structure the key information from the reports for easier analysis and comparison. 5) Report Categorization implement a classi- fication model, such as a fine-tuned BERT or a traditional machine learning classifier, to categorize reports based on their content (normal condition, haemangioma, benign, focal fatty sparing, cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), liver tissue appears ablated, presence of liver metastasis, liver abscess identified, foreign body). As a result, reports are organized into meaningful categories to facilitate pri- oritization and further analysis. 6) Data Visualization generate visual repre- sentations of the extracted data using libraries like Matplotlib or Plotly in Python. This creates easily interpretable summaries of the report findings for quick review by radiologists or referring physicians. 7) Integration with PACSRIS develop inter- faces to

integrate the processed report data with existing Picture Archiving and Communication Sys- tems (PACS) or Radiology Information Systems (RIS). To ensure seamless incorporation of the an- alyzed data into existing clinical workflows. D. Evaluation A comprehensive evaluation will be conducted with a panel of experts in medical informatics and AI technology to optimize the benefits of prompt engineering in processing medical reports. The evalu- ation process focuses on the accuracy of information extraction that can determine and evaluate whether the extracted and categorized information retains Fig. 1 Radiology report processing workflow the clinical significance of the original reports. Mea- sure the accuracy of automated report categorization compared to expert manual classification. Evaluate the ease of integrating existing electronic health record (EHR) systems. Assess the accessibility and usability of the processed information within current clinical workflows. The results of this comprehensive evaluation will guide the refinement and enhance- ment of the medical report processing system, ensur- ing it meets the high standards required for clinical use. By providing an efficient, accurate, and reliable tool for managing medical reports, the goal is to improve patient care and positively contribute to medical research. This system can work on diagnos- ing other diseases. 1) Model Initialization The script begins by initializing a pre-trained model (BioGPT) and its tokenizer using Hugging Faces transformers library, the OpenAI API with the ChatGPT model. BioGPT is a model specifically designed for biomedical text generation tasks. 2) Data preparation The training and evalua- tion datasets are loaded from JSON files in Google Drive. The datasets are tokenized and prepared using a custom class (Radiology Dataset) to ensure com- patibility with the models input requirements. 3) Training Configuration The training argu- ments object defines the parameters for training, such as output directory, number of epochs, and batch size. 4) Model

Training and Evaluation A trainer object is created to handle the model training pro- cess, incorporating the defined arguments, datasets, and tokenizers. 5) SavingtheTrainedModel The trained model is saved to the specified output directory for fu- ture use. This methodology section integrates various prompt engineering techniques to handle complex medical reports. Results In our preliminary evaluation of the AI tools, we conducted a self-evaluation using a sample of medical reports. The study focused on the contri- butions of prompt engineering to healthcare natural language processing applications, including question- answering systems, text summarization, machine translation, and tumor classification. AI techniques demonstrated the capability to adapt to new scenar- ios with minimal labeled data 11. Our experiments involving prompt selection via semantic similarity with GPT4 yielded the most favorable outcomes on the validation split. Prompt selection proved the most effective approach for medical reports and GPT4. The performance of prompt selection is at- tributedtothein-contextmedicalreportsselectedfor semantic similarity with the input dialogue. This ap- proach generated coherent and accurate summaries. A. Experiment 1 (Zero-Shot classification) Fig. 2 The integration of AI technology into tradi- tional radiology reports To classify liver conditions from radiology re- ports containing CT or MRI scans and unstructured doctor notes, we utilized a zero-shot classification model using LLM specifically the Facebookbart- large-mnli model from the Hugging Face transform- ers library. We crafted specific prompts based on extracting the medical report content, focusing on key clinical indicators and findings. Fig. 2 present a comparative analysis between radiological diagnoses and AIML-based classification outcomes

with a high accuracy score in diagnosing liver tumors. The left column shows different medical cases, each accompa- nied by an image from the diagnostic imaging in CT scans or MRIs with a brief description of the results from the radiology report. These results demonstrate how closely the AI models predictions align with the expert radiologists diagnosis, providing a quantita- tive measure of the models accuracy and reliability in interpreting radiological scans. The figure effec- tively highlights both the potential and limitations of AI in detecting diagnostic processes, providing insights into areas where prompt engineering models perform well and where further refinement might be needed to match human expert accuracy. The multi-labelTrue option allows the model to classify the text under multiple categories simultaneously, essential in medical contexts where a patient may have more than one condition Table 1. about post-TACE changes as in Table 2. C. This is done by extracting key clinical findings using regular expres- sions (regex) and generating structured prompts for clinicians to review. The methodology is done by reading PDF reports and converting the unstruc- tured text into a single string for programmatic processing. Use regex to identify and extract key terms related to liver conditions such as lesion loca- tion, steatosis status, cirrhosis status, lesion size, and liver tumor status. Based on the extracted findings, the script generates two types of prompts Discrete Prompts Request the clinician to verify specific find- ings that can address categorical information such as the presence or absence of a tumor as in Table 3. Continuous Prompts Focus on measurements, such as confirming lesion size and tracking changes over time. They facilitate consistent documentation of quantitative data.

Experiment 4 (Fun-tuning prompt engineering) In this experiment, we evaluate the performance using the two advanced medical language models (BioGPT and BioBERT v1.1 (NER)) on the sample of the medical radiology reportsdataset as in Table 7. The evaluation was conducted through many steps, including data preparation, model fine-tuning, and performance measurement across multiple metrics. 1) Installation of Libraries and Preparation of Data We began by installing the necessary libraries, transformers, and sacremoses, to facilitate the use of pre-trained language models and tokenization pro- cesses. This dataset was structured and saved in JSON format to ensure compatibility with the mod- els and ease of use in subsequent training phases. 2) Model and tokenizer uploadTokenizers spe- cific to the medical domain were uploaded to pre- process the text data effectively. We utilized the tokenizers corresponding to BioGPT, BioBERT v1.1 (NER). These models were chosen due to their fine- tuning of extensive biomedical literature, enabling them to handle complex medical language effectively. 3) Dataset preparationThe radiology datasets were loaded and divided into training and evaluation subsets. 4) Performance Evaluation MetricsThe mod- els were fine-tuned using the prepared training dataset, and their performance was evaluated on the validation set using standard metrics. 5) Results StorageThe computed metrics were systematically saved in the Radiology Model

Re- sults directory as shown in Table 4. 6) Visualization of ResultsFacilitating the in- terpretation of the models performance, we plotted the evaluation metrics across epochs. These visual- izations provided insights into the learning dynamics and stability of the models over time, as in Fig. 3 and Table 5. Inputs tokenizer(medical_text, return_tensorspt) NER_outputs model.generate(inputs, max_new_tokens50) NER_result tokenizer.decode(ner_outputs0, skip_special_tokensTrue) We utilized the BioGPT model, a pre-trained language model specifically designed for biomedical text generation, to produce clinically coherent nar- ratives based on patient-doctor notes. The model effectively synthesizes detailed medical information, such as diagnoses and treatment plans. Parameters were optimized to maintain text quality and rele- vance, using temperature, top-k, and top-p sampling methods to balance accuracy and creativity in the generated content. The models demonstrated consistent improve- ment in accuracy and precision across multiple med- ical reports. These results highlight the potential of integrating prompt engineering with LLMs for medical applications. In the concluding section, we discuss the implications of our findings and potential directions for future research. V. Conclusion and future work The evaluation of the AI tool for processing medical reports has demonstrated its potential to en- hance clinical documentation through accurate infor- mation extraction, meaningful categorization, and ef- fective visualization.

While the tool shows promise in improving efficiency and supporting clinical decision- making, further refinement is necessary to address challenges related to consistency, handling complex cases, and seamless integration with existing health- care systems. The use of AI, particularly through prompt engineering, could potentially reduce the workload of clinicians by saving time in review- ing and interpreting reports. However, these savings may vary depending on the complexity of cases and specific workflows in different healthcare settings. Continued development and iterative testing will be essential to fully realizing its benefits in enhanc- ing patient care and advancing medical research. In the future, we can evaluate, debug, and moni- tor chains built by the LLM framework using the Lang Smith application before production 30. Also, we anticipate significant advancements in Retrieval- Augmented Generation (RAG), AI agents, and Col- laborative Robot Workstations (CREW). These de- velopments are expected to enhance the capabilities 191 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111000 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. of AI systems and their integration into medical applications. Additionally, increasing the dataset size by adding more medical reports will be essential. Constructing a Medical Domain Functional Knowledge Graph with Large Language Models Lintian Ji Huzhou Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing and Optimal Control for Industrial Systems School of Engineering, Huzhou University Huzhou, China 18537731956163.com Shuxin Du Huzhou Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing and Optimal Control for Industrial Systems School of Engineering, Huzhou University Huzhou, China Yi Qiu Huzhou Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing and Optimal Control for Industrial Systems School of Engineering, Huzhou University Huzhou, China Hongyi Xu Huzhou Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing and Optimal Control for Industrial Systems School of Engineering, Huzhou University Huzhou, China Xiaorui Guo Huzhou Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing and Optimal Control for Industrial Systems School of Engineering, Huzhou University Huzhou, China AbstractThe integration of medicine and artificial intelligence is creating novel opportunities to gather, analyze, and generate groundbreaking medical insights from the vast expanse of medical literature. Although these advancements hold great promise, difficulties including manual annotation and precise data retrieval, and transparency persist. Advanced natural language processing (NLP) techniques and large language models (LLMs) have shown potential in addressing these issues. This paper introduces the Medical K nowledge Graph (MedKG), a comprehensive and multidisciplinary medical knowledge graph. By utilizing advanced NLP algorithms, MedKG extracts millions of entities and converts them into triples from a corpus of superior medical research papers published over the last decade. It categorizes unstructured medical information into nine distinct tags, including disease names, treatments, diagnoses, symptoms, genetics, cellular structures of diseases, patient data, medical procedures, and treatment applications, while seamlessly integrating the digital object identifier (DOI) of each paper. As the latest structured database of medical knowledge, MedKG serves as a powerful tool for accelerating medical research and lays the groundwork for constructing a more extensive medical knowledge graph using full-text medical articles. Moreover, our research establishes a foundation

for practical knowledge management systems based on text mining, applicable not only to complex medical systems but also to other professional domains. KeywordsMedical e ngineering, NLP, Large language model, Knowledge graph, Database I. INTRODUCTION Mo dern medical research has made significant strides, yet the evolution of medical treatments often depends on traditional, labor-intensive trial-and-error processes combined with clinical expertise. These outdated methods slow down the advancement of cutting-edge medical therapies. The vast and specialized nature of medical science means that researchers in one field may find it challenging to access and interpret knowledge from other areas. For instance, those studying cancer treatments might not fully understand findings related to infectious diseases or neurological conditions 1. Nevertheless, the biological mechanisms underlying various diseases are interrelated, and insights from one field can inform another. There is a pressing need to integrate insights from multiple medical disciplines to speed up research progress. However, essential medical knowledge is scattered across millions of scientific publications, encompassing topics like disease causes, diagnostic methods, treatment strategies, and the study of physiological, biochemical, and genetic factors, as well as their applications in pharmaceuticals, medical devices, and public health. This dispersion of knowledge creates obstacles to cross - disciplinary collaboration and innovation 2. A major shortfall in the current medical research framework is the lack of a comprehensive database that can bring together this dispersed information, making it more accessible and promoting interdisciplinary work. Even though databases like PubMed, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library provide means to search for medical research papers using specific labels, extracting valuable medical information from the extensive body of literature continues to be a formidable challenge . To gain a clearer understanding of medical conditions and treatments, structured databases such as ClinVar, OMIM, and DrugBank have been developed 3. However, these databases

primarily contain data from clinical trials and genetic studies, which may not always fully align with real-world patient outcomes. Thus, the medical field urgently requires a database that leverages real patient data and clinical practice 4. Furthermore, medical information in medicine is not limited to disease diagnosis and treatment options, but also includes patient demographics, comorbidities, and treatment outcomes. For example, patients with chronic conditions often require a personalized treatment plan based on their specific needs, medical history 5, and genetic characteristics. In this context, specific databases focused on patient outcomes and treatment effects have the potential to provide more valuable information to medical professionals and researchers than general medical databases such as PubMed that may not 491 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS) 979-8-3503-7431-52431.00 2024 IEEE July 26-28, 2024 Shenyang, China 2024 IEEE 6th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS) 979-8-3503-7431-52431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109ICPICS62053.2024.10796042 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111815 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. capture the nuances of personalized medicine 6. This highlights the necessity of creating specialized databases to address the specific requirements of medical research and clinical practice , promoting more targeted approaches to disease treatment and research. Knowledge graphs (KGs) are pivotal in the medical domain, offering structured representations of medical information 7, including controlled vocabulary and ontological relations. These graphs, comprised of nodes and edges, enable complex queries and insights crucial for medical research and decision -making. Despite their potential, constructing KGs in the medical field demands extensive input from domain experts due to the intricacies and specialized n ature of medical knowledge 8. This labor - intensive process hampers scalability, impacts performance, and poses challenges in maintaining timely and accurate data. The swift progress

in natural language processing (NLP) has transformed the extraction of information from unstructured medical text, enabling the efficient and precise creation of medical knowledge graphs 9. For example, the development of Medical Conditions Knowledge Graph (MCKG) has showcased the capability to integrate medical information from diverse sources, offering a unified view of medical knowledge, including disease etiology, diagnostic methods, treatment options, and genetic aspects. Specialized medical knowledge graphs such as Cardiovascular Disease Knowledge Graph (CVD -KG) and Neuro logical Disorders Knowledge Graph (ND-KG) have also emerged, focusing on specific medical areas and aiding in potential treatment discoveries. However, constructing and maintaining medical knowledge graphs face significant challenges. Despite reduced dependency on experts with advancements in NLP, extensive annotation of training data remains essential to ensure accuracy and reliability 10. Furthermore, predicting relationships between medical entities to form triples in the KG can sometimes lack authenticity, affecting the credibility of the graph. Updating medical knowledge graphs is another hurdle, as new medical entities require accurate relationship predictions with existing entities, complicating the graphs dynamic and accurate maintenance. To tackle these issues, the appearance of large language models (LLMs) like MedicalBERT and ClinicalBERT presents promising solutions. These LLMs, fine -tuned for medical text task, significantly enhance the extraction and credibility of structured medical informat ion. We adopt such LLMs to improve tasks like Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Relation Extraction (RE) without extensive manual efforts, thus advancing medical knowledge graph construction and utilization in medical research and decision support systems. In this research, we have made significant strides in developing a Medical Knowledge Graph (MedKG), an advanced graph database tailored specifically for medical research and knowledge organization. Our contributions are prominently featured in three main domains (1) We present a novel approach for attaining exceptional precision in named entity recognition (NER), relation

extraction (RE), and entity resolution (ER) within medical texts. This method efficiently converts unstructured medical data into triples while preserving the source information of each triplet, enabling convenient updates to the MedKG. (2) We constructed a medical knowledge graph (MedKG) containing partial medical information. MedKG provides healthcare professionals and medical researchers with access to comprehensive medical knowledge through inquiry, facilitating in -depth exploration and analysis. (3) We implemented a well- defined labeling system in MedKG to enhance scalability and facilitate potential integration with other disciplines structured databases or knowledge graphs. II. M ETHODS A. Experimental principle The comprehensive workflow of our medica l research is shown in Figure 1. We utilize Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Relation Extraction (RE) tasks to derive structured information about disease information, treatments, and drug composition. Subsequently, entity resolution (ER) and standardization processes are applied to integrate information from these domains into a cohesive medical knowledge graph. Figure 1. Workflow chart The workflow outlined in Figure 2 begins with manual annotation and normalization of an initial training dataset, followed by fine -tuning of a large language model (LLM) specifically tailored for NER and RE tasks related to pharmaceutical entities. The resulting inference dataset is partitioned into ten segments, an essential phase for the following iterative procedure. Next, ER tasks are performed using natural language processing (NLP) techniques such as ChemDataExtractor, mat2vec, and in-house developed expert dictionaries. Following ER, superior outcomes are thoroughly curated to expand the training dataset , th us improving the models effectiveness in future versions. Figure 2. Pipeline of the fine-tuned LLM for knowledge graph tasks Ultimately, the medical knowledge graph is formed using the extracted trios from the normalized results obtained after the final iteration. This systematic approach ensures the integration of disparate medical information into a structured and accessible k

nowledge graph, thereby facilitating comprehensive analysis and research in the medical field. 492 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Data preparation and schema design Medical experts carefully annotated 9 different categories from 55 medical research paper abstracts as a training dataset for a large language model (LLM). These categories include basic tags such as disease name, treatment, or prescription, which, although in different formats, always represent specific medical concepts in different papers. Additionally, supplemental label s include Symptom Description, Biological StructureFunction, Clinical Application, Patient Outcomes, Treatment Methods, and Diagnostic Methods. The Patient Outcomes tab summarizes quantitative results such as Survival, Recovery Time, and Mortality, while the Symptom Description tab contains qualitative descriptions such as Acute, Severe, and Severe. Taking into account the practical aspects of medical applications, we have also annotated under the Patient Outcomes tab essential parameters that are directly relevant to a specific Clinical Application. For example, attributes such as the efficacy rate of vaccines, the operative success rate of surgeries, and the duration of pain relief of drugs are systematically annotated. And the central node Medical Entity is crucial, connected to nodes describing its nomenclature, composition and various properties. Specifically, medical entity includes medical name, disease name and abbreviation. Biological structurefunction describes its anatomical or physiological aspects clinical application refers to its actual medical use and patient outcomes outlines its impact on patient health. The Medical Entity node also links to the Symptom Description node which provi des additional qualitative information. In addition, the Clinical Application node branches into Patient Outcomes, Symptom Descriptions, and Medical Domains, Suggesting additional specifications and contextual relevance for medical applications. To identify the origins of nodes and relationships, each node is associated with a Digital Object Identifier

(DOI) node. By examining the shared DOI node neighbors at both ends of a relationship, we can pinpoint the source article from which the relationship originated. Considering the complexity of medical sentences and the variety of terms used in abstracts, a normalization process was applied following the initial extraction. This standardization is intended to provide a consistent representation of entities with similar meanings in medical texts. For instance, terms like cardiovascular disease and heart disease were standardized to cardiovascular disease, while terms such as surgical intervention approach, postoperative care approach, and therapy-based approach were simplified to treatment procedure. . Undergoing this normalization process, all annotated entities achieve consistency, which in turn facilitates the efficient training of language models (LLMs). We have collected 100,000 abstra cts of medically relevant peer -reviewed research articles from authoritative medical databases, each abstract is organized in JSON format with a DOI - text structure to facilitate smooth processing and thorough analysis. C. LLMs training, evaluation and inference The training dataset includes aggregated information used for fine-tuning models such as BioMedLM, LLama2 7B, and ChatGLM 6B. After achieving high -quality outcomes from normalized inference, we repeatedly retrained the fine-tuned LLM to predict following data sets. The models were trained for 8 iterations with a single-sample batch size. Furthermore, we labeled 50 abstracts to evaluate the LLMs performance. Our assessment focuses on the models Named Entity Recognition (NER) capabilities and explores its ability to identify potential internal relationships among sets of related elements in the Relationship Extraction (RE) task. Furthermore, during the data compilation phase, we standardized entities and evaluated the effectiveness of the LLMs in this standardization process. For tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Relation Extraction (RE), and Entity Resolution (ER), our performance evaluation utilized a comprehensive framework, focusing on metrics such as accuracy,

sensitivity, and harm onic mean, while considering errors like incorrect positives and missed detections. In Entity Resolution (ER), An accurate positive denotes the exact alignment of an entity with the given schema. On the other hand, a false positive (FP) happens when the model incorrectly detects an entity, relationship, or standardization, while an overlooked positive (FN) s ignifies the models failure to recognize a correct entity, relation, or schema element. Based on these definitions, we can assess each task using Equations (1), (2), and (3) . After performing the evaluation, we select a fine- tuned Large Language Model (LLM) that shows optimal performance in NER and RE tasks to process 100,000 abstracts iteratively. The results of these inferences are formatted as DOI - text - response. Thus, the fine-tuned LLM performs both entity recognition and categorization, achieving NER and RE tasks simultaneously. Additionally, all identified entities and relationships in the output are trackable, improving data integrity and usability. D. Data matching The accuracy and reliability of a Medical Knowledge Graph (KG) are paramount, necessitating a meticulous review and correction of inference results before constructing the graph. To standardize core medical labels, we implement a two-step process ER-NFA to ER-NF. During the ER-NFA phase, we utilize specialized medical extraction too ls to extract medical terms and their corresponding

acronyms from abstracts. Subsequently, we employ the MedNLP model for utilizing word embeddings to compare entities identified by both Language Models (LLMs) and extraction tools to correct and ensure the precision and accuracy of Name and Acronym correspondence. However, the classification of medical terms and their formulas is often inaccurate and challenging to discern using standard extraction tools. Hence, we perform ER-NF by utilizing 1500 accurately classified labels as a training set to fine -tune the LLM for binary 493 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111815 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. classification. Furthermore, we choose an extra 150 labels to assess how well the dichotomous classification performs. For normalizing objects and connections in other labels (ER-OL), we employ clustering of word embeddings to create a specialized medical lexicon . This involves converting medical terms into vector form using MedNLP and clustering semantically similar words based on vector similarity. Specifically, we create a method for dynamic vector clustering that is based on density and dynamically forms clusters without predefined cluster numbers. This adaptive clustering method employs a threshold of similarity to establish group memberships, allowing medical entities to join or form clusters according to their closeness in feature spaces, fundamentally adapting to the inherent structure of medical data. In the domain of medical literature analysis, we employed domain experts to categorize clusters, yielding approximately 500 key terms. These terms encompass diverse aspects such as anatomical structures, physiological phases, clinical applications, diagnostic methods, therapeutic modalities, patient outcomes, and disease descriptions. Subsequently, we applied analytical methodologies to integrate this expert - derived vocabulary with entities identified through advanced natural language processing techniques using MedNLP . Following a thorough similarity comparison process, we completed the standardization to ensure the accuracy of both entities and their

relationships. It is noteworthy that the scope of Characteristics and Attributes labels often extends beyond specific medical domains and may include a wide range of related information. Consequently, we implemented a relatively flexible standardization approach for these labels, focusing on aligning content while retaining unmatched entities and relations. To enhance the quality of our training dataset, we meticulously selected a subset of high -quality data from the normalized inference results of each iterative process, integrating this data into our training set. This high -quality Data is characterized by its precision and recall measurements, ensuring the robustness of our training regimen. Furthermore, we conducted a rigorous evaluation of the fine-tuned natural language model, trained on progressively refined datasets, to ensure optimal performance in analyzing and interpreting medical literature. E. Knowledge graph construction To organize the deduced outcomes into triples in the medical domain, we utilize three labels that denote the medical concept as the primary tag, where it serves as the main component, the names of other labels constitute the relationships, and their values represent the associated details. Within the structure of primary labels, Symptom is given the highest priority, followed by Disease, and then Treatment. Additionally, each main concept and its associated details are linked to the Digital Object Identifier (DOI) of the source article from which the information was extracted. This linkage enables us to trace the origin of the relationship between any two concepts by examining the overlap in their associated entities. Subsequently, we convert the triples into a Medical Knowledge Graph (MedKG) and store it using the Neo4j graph database, which also supports subgraph matching functionality. This feature aligns well with the requirement to search for specific medical concepts based on user -input criteria. To enhance accessibility to the comprehensive data, we also offer the data set

available in CSV format for convenient information management. III. E XPERIMENTAL RESULTS In Table I , we present a performance comparison of Language Model Models (LLMs) including BioMedLM, ChatGLM 6B, and LLaMA2 in Named Entity Recognition (NER), Relationship Extraction (RE) and Entity Matching (ER) tasks within the medical domain. BioMedLM demonstrates significantly higher F1 scores in both Named Entity Recognition (NER) and RE tasks compared to the ChatGLM 6B and LLaMA2 7b models, indicating its superior performance in text analysis tasks related to medicine. However, there is no notable variation in their efficacy for ER activities among these models, we attribute this observation to the limited contextual memory capabilities of the LLMs . To address this challenge, we applied entity resolution to the inference outputs, resulting in substantial improvements in the Normalization (ER) activities. This enhancement method has also enhanced the p erformance in NER and Relation RE activities. The enhancements are due to the ER tasks capability to correct erroneous inferences caused by hallucinations and to further refine the derived connections. TABLE I. Figure 3. Local schematic diagram of MedKG IV. CONCLUSION In this research, we introduce a novel

natural language processing (NLP) pipeline tailored for constructing knowledge graphs (KGs) aimed at efficiently extracting triplets extracted from unstructured scientific text. The unique aspect of this method lies in its capacity to fine -tune a large language model (LLM) with minimal data annotation. This enables the fine-tuned LLM to extract structured information from vast amounts of unstructured text. By bypassing predictive algorithms, this approach ensures the highest level of authenticity and traceability for the structured information. Utilizing this technique, we created an operational medicine knowledge graph (MedKG) containing medical material and related knowledge sourced from abstracts of 100,000 peer - reviewed papers. Subsequent analysis validated the effectiveness and credibility of MedKG. Our methodology and the resulting knowledge graph possess considerable potential across multiple dimensions. Firstly, by deepening the extraction of structured data, including entire research articles, we aim to create a more thorough and comprehensive knowledge graph. This entails not only broadening the range of data analysis but also enhancing methods to encapsulate subtleties in intricate academic literature. Furthermore, improving identifiers for entities in our framework facilitates finer data classification, including the addition of granular attributes like preparation parameters or precise parameters . This enhancement substantially increases the level of detail and usefulness of the knowledge graph. In addition, the flexibility of our NLP process demonstrates its adaptability across various scientific fields, providing a foundation for building specialized knowledge networks. Finally, integrating MedKG integrating with existing knowledge graphs enables the creation of interconnected and holistic datasets, fostering cutting -edge research and application development in medical science and other fields. Wonju, Republic of Korea evanbreathings.co.kr Hwamin Lee Department of Biomedical Informatics Korea University College of Medicine Seoul, Republic of Korea hwaminkorea.ac.kr Abstract Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are vital for diagnosing various pulmonary conditions, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma. Traditional PFTs, conducted using laboratory -based spirometers, are accurate but costly and require s killed technicians. Recent advancements in portable spirometry and large language models (LLMs) offer promising alternatives for remote diagnostics and clinical decision support. This study evaluates the performance of three advanced LLMs Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet in understanding and interpreting PFTs data. The models were assessed using three prompt types zero shot, guidelines enhanced, and few shot, and their performance was measured in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and processing speed. Results indicate that Claude 3.5 Sonnet consistently outperformed the other models across all metrics, demonstrating superior comprehension and classification abilities. Error analysis revealed specific areas for improvemen t, particularly in logical reasoning and adherence to guidelines. The findings highlight the potential of LLMs to enhance diagnostic processes and reduce healthcare costs, while also emphasizing the need for further research to address data

privacy, intero perability, and ethical considerations for clinical integration. Future efforts should focus on leveraging open-source models and expanding datasets to optimize LLMs for real-world medical applications. Keywords Chain of Thought, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Clinical Rationale Generation, Large Language Models, Medical Guidelines Prompt I. INTRODUCTION Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are essential diagnostic tools used to assess lung health and diagnose various pulmonary conditions, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), asthma, and restrictive lung diseases 1-3. The Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) report highlights that COPD is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, necessitating accurate diagnostic methods 4. Traditionally, PFTs are conducted using laboratory -based spirometers, which prov ide precise measurements but come with high costs and require skilled technicians. This makes the approach impractical and leads to substantial increases in medical expenses for patients directed to tertiary hospitals 5. Several studies have indicated that measurements obtained using portable spirometers are nearly identical to those from traditional, laboratory-based spirometers 6, 7. This suggests that portable spirometers, with their cost -effectiveness, portability, and ease of use, can serve as a viable alternative to conventional PFTs devices 8. Recently, the integration of portable spirometry with mobile applications has enabled patients to easily measure their lung capacity at home and transmit the results to hea lthcare providers. This advancement goes beyond the development of remote treatment plans, extending to the creation of diverse software services related to pulmonary health 9-11. Moreover, with the advent of large language models (LLMs), the medical field has seen substantial advancements 12, including the development of medical domain LLMs capable of passing medical licensing exams 13, as well as applications in disease predic tion and diagnostic consultation chatbots 14. Ongoing research is also aimed at creating LLMs that can perform tasks beyond simple algorithmic

classification of pulmonary function test data, generating pulmonary function test charts at the proficiency le vel of medical residents 15. Such innovations have the potential to significantly reduce unnecessary medical expenditures and alleviate the burden of frequent hospital visits for patients. This research was supported by the BioMedical Technology Development Program of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. RS-2024-00440371) and by the NRF grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. RS-2024-00457381). 2024 IEEE EMBS International Conference on Biomedical and Health Informatics (BHI) 979-8-3503-5155-22431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109BHI62660.2024.10913702 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 112445 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. Therefore, the aim of this study is to conduct foundational research by evaluating and comparing the comprehension of pulmonary function test data among three leading state -of-the- art large parameter LLMs, namely the Gemini 1.5 Pro 16, the GPT 4o 17 an d the Claude 3.5 Sonnet 18, which have demonstrated top tier performance across various assessments. II. METHODOLOGY The methodology section provides a comprehensive overview of the experimental design, data collection, preprocessing, and the specific approaches used to evaluate the performance of three advanced LLMs in interpreting PFTs data. A. Data Collection and Preprocessing The study involved a total of 200 participants who were recruited from various primary healthcare settings. Participants included individuals across different age groups, genders, and varying health statuses to ensure a representative sample. Each participant provided informed consent before participating in the study, ensuring ethical standards were maintained. Data were collected using the portable spirometer BULO M, developed by BREATHINGS Co., Ltd. This device is known for its accuracy and portability, which makes it ideal for use in both clinical and home settings. The spirometer measured two key pulmonary parameters forced

vital capacity (FVC) and forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1). These parameters are critical in diagnosis of pulmonary diseases. The predicted normal values for FVC and FEV1, used to classify pulmonary disease types, were calculated using Dr. Chois equation 19, a widely accepted formula for predicting normal values based on the Asian population and considering age, sex, height, and weight. This normalization was essential to account for individual variations and ensure that the comparisons made across participants were valid. Using the predicted normal values for FVC and FEV1, the data were classified into four pulmonary disease categories normal, restrictive, obstructive, or combined. The classification followed established diagnostic guidelines Normal FEV1FVC ratio of 70 or higher and FVC 80 or higher of the predicted normal value for FVC labeled as normal. Restrictive FEV1FVC ratio of 70 or higher and FVC less than 80 of the predicted normal value for FVC labeled as restrictive. Obstructive FEV1FVC ratio of less than 70 and FVC 80 or higher of the predicted normal value for FVC labeled as obstructive. Combined Meeting the criteria for both restrictive and obstructive conditions labeled as combined. The ground truth labels for each participant were determined based on the above contents. To facilitate the evaluation of the LLMs, the numerical PFTs data were transformed into standardized sentences. These sentences followed a specific structure, as outlined in the studys guidelines, to ensure consistency and clarity. Each sentence included the measured FVC and FEV1 values and the predicted normal values for these measurements to maintain uniformity. B. Large Language Models The study evaluated three state of the art LLMs Gemini 1.5 Pro (developed by Google), GPT 4o (developed by OpenAI), and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (developed by Anthropic). These models were chosen based on their superior performance across various natural languag e

processing tasks and their availability via application programming interfaces. Each model was accessed through its respective application programming interface and configured with specific parameters to ensure consistent performance across all tests Temperature Set to 0.0 to ensure deterministic outputs. This parameter controls the randomness of the models predictions, with a lower temperature resulting in more consistent and predictable responses. Top p Set to 1.0, which means the entire probability distribution of possible tokens was considered during the generation of responses. This setting ensures that no potential answers are excluded from consideration. C. Prompts for Pulmonary Disease Type Classification Prompt engineering is a crucial aspect of evaluating LLMs, as the quality and structure of prompts can significantly impact the models performances. This study employed three types of prompts to assess the models abilities to interpret PFTs data and generate accurate clinical rationales. Each case was processed in a separate session to ensure that prior inputs did not influence subsequent responses, maintaining the integrity and independence of each test case. Zero shot prompting involves providing the model with PFTs data without any additional context or examples. This type of prompt assesses the models inherent ability to understand and interpret the data based solely on its pre -existing knowledge. Each model was given a prompt structured as follows, corresponding to Fig. 1 Entry of transformed PFTs data. A request to generate a diagnosis (normal, restrictive, obstructive, or combined). A request to provide a clinical rationale in four sentences. To improve the models performance, zero shot prompt were enhanced with specific diagnostic guidelines and the chain of thought (CoT) technique 20. The guidelines provided explicit criteria for classifying pulmonary diseases, while the CoT technique involved breaking down the reasoning process into sequential steps. The guidelines enhanced prompt structure included the following, corresponding to Fig.

1 The same PFTs data as provided in the zero shot prompt. Detailed diagnostic guidelines to assist the model in making accurate classifications. CoT sentences to guide the model through the reasoning process step by step. Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 112445 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. Fig. 1. Prompts for classification of pulmonary disease types and generation of clinical rationale Few shot prompting involves providing the model with a small number of example cases in addition to the primary prompt. This approach helps the model understand the task better by learning from the examples. For this study, four example datasets were included in the prompts. The structure of the few shot prompt, as shown in Fig. 1, was as follows The same PFTs data input as the previous two prompts. The same diagnostic guidelines and CoT sentences input as the previous prompts. Input of four example responses. D. Evaluation for Classification The quantitative performance of the LLMs in classifying different types of pulmonary diseases was assessed through a variety of evaluation metrics Accuracy The proportion of correct predictions out of the total predictions. Precision The ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false positives, indicating the models ability to avoid false positives and its reliability in positive predictions. Recall The ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives, reflecting the models ability to identify all relevant instances and its effectiveness in capturing actual positives. F1 Score The harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced measure of the models performance, particularly useful when there is an uneven class distribution. These metrics were selected to ensure a comprehensive understanding of how well the models performed in distinguishing between the four categories

of pulmonary conditions. E. Error Types The clinical rationales generated by the LLMs were analyzed for different types of errors, including Comparison Operation Errors (COE) These errors occurred when the models logical operations involving numerical comparisons were flawed, despite the calculations being correct. Guideline Deviations (GD) These errors happened when the model deviated from the provided diagnostic Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 112445 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. guidelines, either by offering alternative explanations or additional clinical opinions that were not aligned with the guidelines. Misdiagnoses (MD) In these cases, the model generated a clinical rationale that aligned with the diagnostic guidelines, but the final diagnosis was incorrect. Miscalculations (MC) These errors involved incorrect calculations during the reasoning process, which could lead to incorrect conclusions. III. RESULTS In this section, we present the detailed results of our evaluation of the three LLMs Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, on their comprehension of PFTs data. The evaluation was conducted across three types of prompts zero shot, guidelines enha nced, and few shot. This includes the quantitative performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, along with the time efficiency and the number of tokens generated by the three models for 200 iterations to assess their processing speed and efficiency under different prompt types. Additionally, we present a comprehensive error analysis to provide insights into the models capabilities and limitations. A. Quantitative Performance and Time Efficiency of Pulmonary Disease Type Classification by Prompt The zero shot performance of the models was evaluated by providing them with only the PFTs results without any additional context. Table I summarizes the accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score for each model across the four pulmonary disease types normal, restrictive, obstructive, and combined.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet outperformed the other models in the zero shot setting across all disease types, achieving the highest accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. This indicates that Claude 3.5 Sonnet has a superior inherent ability to understand and classify pulmonary diseases based solely on the provided PFTs data. TABLE I. C. Error Rate In this study, we conducted a detailed error analysis to understand the types and frequencies of errors made by Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet in interpreting PFTs data. Fig. 2 illustrates the error rate for each model across the different prompt types (zero shot, guidelines enhanced, and few shot). The results reveal that Claude 3.5 Sonnet consistently outperformed both Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT 4o in all prompt settings, achieving the lowest error rate. In the zero shot setting, Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved an error rate of 20, significantly lower than GPT 4o (26) and Gemini 1.5 Pro (45). This indicates that Claude 3.5 Sonnet has a superior inherent ability to understand and classify pulmonary disease types based on PFTs data wit hout additional context. Fig. 2. Error rate in the classification of pulmonary disease types The introduction of diagnostic guidelines and the CoT technique resulted in improved performance for all models. Claude 3.5 Sonnet again demonstrated the lowest error rate at 19, followed by GPT 4o at 27 and Gemini 1.5 Pro at 52. Despite the addition of guidelines, the error rate for both Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT 4o slightly increased. The inclusion of four example datasets further reduced the error rate across all

models. Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved the lowest error rate at 16, GPT 4o improved to 18, and Gemini 1.5 Pro showed a notable reduction to 28. The few shot learning approach s ignificantly enhanced the models ability to leverage additional context, resulting in more accurate classifications. D. Types of Errors in Few Shot Prompt Fig. 3 categorizes the examples of errors generated by the LLMs into four types. COE referred to instances where the calculations were correct, but the logical operation involving comparisons with values such as 70 or 80 was flawed. GD indicated cases where the LLMs deviated from the provided diagnostic guidelines during the second and third prompts, providing alternative explanations or additional clinical opinions. MD occurred when the clinical rationale aligns with the diagnostic guidelines, but the diagnosis field contained an incorrect answer. Lastly, MC denoted calculation errors, which were observed only in the Gemini 1.5 Pro model. These errors cooccurred with 3 instances of MD, 6 instances of GD, and 10 instances of COE. Fig. 4 illustrates the number of errors by type generated by each model for the few shot prompt. The Gemini 1.5 Pro model displayed a balanced distribution of errors, including COE, GD, and MD, as well as calculation mistakes. The GPT 4o model exhibited relati vely fewer errors overall, most of which were of the COE type. The Claude 3.5 Sonnet model had the fewest errors overall, with the most common type being GD, indicating its tendency to provide additional clinical insights. This indicates that while the model had some understanding of the guidelines, it frequently made logical errors and provided incorrect diagnoses despite correct rationales, compounded further by instances of MCs. The GPT 4o, with relatively fewer errors, showed a predominant issue with COEs, suggesting that the models main challenge was in the logical interpretation of numerical data rather than following the guidelines or providing accurate diagnoses. The tendency of Claude 3.5 Sonnet to deviate slightly from the guidelines while still offering clinically relevant insights indicates its advanced understandin g of the context, albeit with a propensity to provide more information than necessary. IV. DISCUSSION The results highlight the potential of advanced LLMs, particularly the Claude 3.5 Sonnet, in the medical field for diagnosing pulmonary diseases. Although Claude 3 Opus is a different model from the Claude 3.5 Sonnet and is not specialized for the medical domain, it demonstrated performance 21 on the PubMedQA dataset 22 nearly equivalent to Googles Med-PaLM 2 23, which is designed specifically for medical applications. This suggests that the contexts of our experiment and those results are similar. The ability of these models to accurately interpret PFTs data and generate clinical rationales can significantly enhance diagnostic processes 24, reduce healthcare costs 25, and improve patient outcomes 26, 27. Integrating such AI technologies into c linical settings can provide healthcare professionals with reliable decision support tools 28, leading to more efficient and accurate diagnoses 29, and potentially advancing to specific tasks such as summarizing risk levels based on preoperative pulmonary function test results 30. However, implementing LLMs in clinical settings requires careful consideration of data privacy

and integration with existing systems 31. Key steps include ensuring data security through local deployment and anonymization, as well as complying with health care regulations like the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and establishing interoperability with electronic health record (EHR) systems using a standardized application programming interface (API) 32, 33. Additionally, fine tuning LLMs with domain specific data and incorporating human oversight are crucial for enhancing accuracy and maintaining clinical standards 34. Therefore, balancing technological advancements with ethical and practical considerations will be critical for the successful integration of LLMs in healthcare 35. Furthermore, for the future development of LLMs capable of generating pulmonary function test charts for use in real world medical settings, it is essential to utilize open sourced LLMs 36. Related research has demonstrated the application of large parameter LLMs, such as GPT 4, to clinical data for diagnosing Alzheimers disease and generating clinical Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 112445 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. rationales, subsequently using knowledge distillation to train open sourced models 37. The key aspect is that large parameter LLMs generate instruction following data for training open sourced models 38. This studys significance lies in comparing three state of the art models, which currently demonstrate superior performance, using PFTs data, and analyzing their errors to provide insights into the understanding of pulmonary diseases by large parameter LLMs. Additionally, exploring and fine tuning other open sourced LLMs can help identify models with even greater potential for clinical applications 39. Further efforts should focus on minimizing specific types of errors, such as COEs and GDs, to enhance the models reliability and accuracy 40. V. CONCLUSION This study evaluated the performance of three state of the art LLMs Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude

3.5 Sonnet in interpreting PFTs data and generating clinical rationales for diagnosing pulmonary diseases. Notably, there are no recent studies that directly compare the performance of these latest models on specific clinical tasks, making this research a novel contribution to the field. The Claude 3.5 Sonnet consistently outperformed the Gemini 1.5 Pro and the GPT 4o across all evaluation metrics and prompt types. It demonstrated the highest accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, indicating its superior ability to understand and class ify PFTs data. The models performance was enhanced by the inclusion of diagnostic guidelines and few shot examples, showcasing its capacity to learn effectively from additional context. The Gemini 1.5 Pro and the GPT 4o also showed improvements with enhan ced prompts, but their overall performance remained below that of the Claude 3.5 Sonnet. From the perspective of error analysis, the Gemini 1.5 Pro exhibited a balanced distribution of errors, including COEs, GDs, and MDs, indicating a need for better logical reasoning and adherence to guidelines. The GPT 4o, while demonstrating fewer errors o verall, struggled primarily with COEs, suggesting a need for improved numerical interpretation capabilities. While this study provides valuable insights, it is limited by the sample size and the specific models evaluated. Future research should involve larger and more diverse datasets to better generalize the findings. Additionally, further efforts should focus on minimizing specific types of errors, such as COEs and GDs, to enhance the models reliability and accuracy. REFERENCES 1 J. M. Haynes, D. A.
