Column Data shows the different types of data used, (i.e., raw and cleansed). The performance of these approaches is evaluated for precision (P), recall (R) and F1 score for top one, five and ten matches. and hyponyms. These findings highlight the importance of the cleansing process in reducing narrative length and improving the clarity of terms. However, they also reveal potential risks associated with content loss or alteration. To address this, we

ensure transparency and reliability through manual evaluation by a domain expert. AI in clinical settings should assist, not replace, human decision-making, with rigorous validation and testing to catch errors and ensure reliability. Subsequently, the datasets underwent MCN using three distinct approaches dictionary matching, the bi-encoder method, and the RAG for re-ranking. Table 2 details the performance of both raw and cleaned corpora across the two custom terminologies (i.e, (UMLS_DE) and (IT_DE)) and approaches. Cleansing text with GPT-4 often improves precision and recall, leading to higher F1 scores when compared to using raw text in all three approaches. Moving from dictionary matching to the bi-encoder approach showed a significant 91.25 increase in the F1 score, indicating a substantial performance improvement using the text cleaned by GPT-4 and the IT_DE terminology. Notably, the unsupervised bi-encoder method using GPT-4-cleaned text achieved an F1 score of 0.568 for top 1 matches, 0.735 for top 5 matches and 0.754 for top 10 matches. Furthermore, transitioning from the bi-encoder to the RAG resulted in a smaller but notable 6.87 gain in F1 score for the top 1 matches, improving from 0.568 to 0.607, demonstrating continued enhancement in performance within the same setting for data and terminology. The re-ranked terms have been cross-checked using an algorithm to ensure that no changes were made to the list of terms other than their order, thereby ensuring that no hallucinations occurred. The IT_DE terminology consistently shows higher performance metrics compared to UMLS_DE. This observation, combined with the performance improvements, highlights the efficacy of these methods in enhancing the efficiency in MCN systems. A. ERROR ANAL YSIS We performed a qualitative error analysis on the top ten candidates retrieved by the best-performing scenario, i.e. bi- encoder on the cleansed corpus using the custom terminology IT_DE. Typical errors are described in the

following. We dis- tinguish between document-cleansing errors and concept- matching errors. Out of the 660 terms, for the top 10 matches, 25.6 (169660) were wrong matches, of which 35.5 (60169) were due to document cleansing. 1) DOCUMENT CLEANSING ERRORS These errors affected the text in a way that the meaning of the cleansed text was altered. E.g., the meaning (in English) of Abdomen soft abdominal wall, no tenderness, intense bowel sounds, no flank pain can be unambiguously obtained from the following passage in the German raw text Abd. BD weich, kein DS, DGs rege, NL frei.. Cleansing transformed this to Abdomen Bauchdecke weich, kein Druckschmerz, Darmgeräusche rege, Leber nicht vergrößert, where NL frei (no flank pain, annotated in the gold standard with 300447004 Kidney non-tender (situation)), was erroneously rephrased to Leber nicht vergrößert (liver not enlarged). A similar phenomenon characterises the next example, which means in EnglishThe cardiac activity is rhythmical, with normal sinus rhythm and appears in raw text as HA rhythmisch, nc.. A cardiologist understands from the context that nc. is the abbreviation of normocard, which justifies the annotation of the whole passage with 64730000 Normal sinus rhythm (finding). Cleansing transformed this passage to Herzaktivität ist rhythmisch, normal korrigiert, which ended up being annotated by the system with 263699000 Cardiac activity (observable entity). This is not wrong, like in the previous example, but not specific enough. The (non-existing) expansion of nc. to normal korrigiert was fortunately not annotated by the 147986 VOLUME 12, 2024 A. Abdulnazar et al. LLMs for Clinical Text Cleansing Enhance Medical Concept Normalization system, but clearly shows the tendency toward hallucination when the language model encounters unknown expressions in uncommon contexts. When analyzing the document cleansing errors, 42 were due to incorrect expansion of short forms, while 20 were caused by adding incorrect substances

to drug names. 2) MAPPING ERRORS These were frequent errors, independent of the cleansing process. So had Mäßig inhomogene Parenchymstruktur der Leber wie bei Steatose LPS. (which means Moderately heterogeneous parenchymal structure of the liver as in steato- sis Liver Parenchymal Steatosis) the gold standard anno- tation 197321007 Steatosis of liver (disorder), because the annotator concluded from the context that steatosis here means steatosis of the liver. However, the system mapped it to the parent concept 1187537008 Steatosis (disorder), along with 127879008 Structure of parenchyma of liver (body structure). This reveals a fundamental problem of compositional terminologies, viz. that different sequences of pre-coordinated concepts can be derived from the same atomic elements. V. DISCUSSION A. IMPACT OF DOCUMENT CLEANSING Similar to the study by Ayre et al. 53, our cleansing process resulted in substantial reductions in word and line counts, cf. Table 1. This suggests that redundant, unnecessary, or irrelevant content was removed, which was likely to enhance text clarity and conciseness. Despite variations in the extent of reduction among different narratives, there was a consistent trend toward a more concise and structured information presentation. However, in some cases, the word number increased, suggesting restructuring for clarity improvement. A typical case is the expansion of acronyms. Across all methods and for both terminology sources, a notable performance improvement occurred when- ever text was cleansed by GPT-4. This suggests that this task successfully approximated the medical terms to more common, normalized terms corresponding to the terminology collections used, leading to improved normalization results. B. EFFECTIVENESS OF DICTIONARY MATCHING Dictionary matching, in isolation, achieved the lowest performance, especially when applied to raw text. This underscores the limitations of a simple matching approach, particularly with clinical texts known for their special jargon that is often not covered by standard terminologies. C. BI-ENCODER METHOD

PERFORMANCE This method consistently outperformed the baseline across all metrics and for both terminology sources, underscoring its MCN effectiveness. Furthermore, the result demonstrates the significant advantage when applied to the (large) interface terminology (IT_DE) over the much smaller UMLS Metathesaurus extract (UMLS_DE). This finding strongly supports the critical importance of good terminology coverage in MCN. D. IMPACT OF GPT-4 re-ranker The GPT-4 re-ranker, in addition to the bi-encoder method, further enhanced performance, particularly on already cleansed text. As per the study by Ma et al. 63, LLMs as re-rankers in challenging information extraction tasks exhib- ited an F1 gain around 2.4. This observation underscores the effective refinement of candidate concepts generated by the bi-encoder, leading to more precise normalization outcomes. To the best of our knowledge, LLMs as re-rankers have not been previously utilized for MCN tasks. Our investigation thus demonstrates a notable improvement, achieving a 6.87 F1 gain compared to the bi-encoder approach alone. A previous attempt to perform MCN directly by prompting GPT-4 for finding the right SNOMED code for a given expression had been immediately abandoned due to its propensity to the suggestion of completely halluci- nated codes, indicating that GPT-4 lacks sufficient medical terminology knowledge. This highlights the necessity for a framework, such as bi-encoders, that first retrieves the correct concepts from the terminology and then improves accuracy by re-ranking. E. THE IMPORTANCE OF TERMINOLOGIES The result provides interesting insight when comparing UMLS_DE with IT_DE, two very different term collections with links to SNOMED CT codes. The former is of limited size (79,000 biomedical terms for 41,000 concepts) and only linked to SNOMED CT indirectly, whereas the latter is huge (2.5 million biomedical terms for 278,000 concepts) and provides good coverage of the numerous varieties of clinical jargon. This explains the almost doubling of the F-values

across experiments. However, the high terminological coverage also explains that IT_DE benefitted comparatively less from document cleansing. It is, however, not surprising that document cleansing is the more beneficial, the scarcer is the coverage by existing terminologies. This is an important message for other languages, for which UMLS terminology extracts similar to our UMLS_DE could easily be obtained, but for which resources like IT_DE do not exist. F. THE INTERPRETATION OF THE BEST-PERFORMING SCENARIO The combination of IT_DE with text cleansing, bi-encoder and re-ranking yields an optimal F11 value of 0.607. This is all the more remarkable when compared to results of an earlier human text annotation exercise with SNOMED CT, where only low inter-annotator agreement values (Krippen- dorffs Alpha approx. 40) had been achieved on a mix of clinical texts 70, and which had been interpreted as a result of SNOMED CTs huge size and of the unclear meaning of semantically related concepts. VOLUME 12, 2024 147987 A. Abdulnazar et al. LLMs for Clinical Text Cleansing Enhance Medical Concept Normalization G. PATIENT SAFETY While challenges such as dealing with medical terminology, correctly resolving brand names, and preventing hallucina- tions remain, the proposed method faces risks such as loss or alteration of content at a relatively low level, given the black-box nature of LLMs. Their potential impact on patient safety must be assessed individually for each implementation scenario, which is, however, beyond the scope of this study. This means, particularly, the adaptation of the weighting between false positives and false negatives to the respective scenario. For instance, false positives may be more acceptable in cohort-building use cases for patient recruitment than in decision support scenarios, where a hallucinated drug or disease code can put the patient at risk. In contrast, when LLMs are used to improve content retrieval in medical

records, a high recall is to be aimed at and false positives are more readily tolerated. H. DATA PROTECTION For our study, we chose GPT-4 was as the currently best-performing language model. However, the use of AI in healthcare requires strict compliance with data protection and security regulations, which makes GPT-4s proprietary nature a severe obstacle. Open source models such as Llama 3 should be preferred, but they have so far had significantly lower performance, especially for languages other than English. So it remains to wait for open source models with better performance. The deployment of commercial LLMs on trusted environments, such as Azure Cloud, could be an alternative. The extent to which this actually ensures the protection of highly sensitive patient data will be the subject of future discussions, as will all the trends that are currently observed in the extremely dynamic landscape of language models. VI. CONCLUSION Our study addressed medical concept normalization (MCN) of clinical narratives, i.e., the automated annotation of clini- cally relevant text passages with codes from the terminology SNOMED CT. The results support the transformative benefits of integrating LLMs such as ChatGPT into a concept nor- malization workflow. More precisely, ChatGPT significantly enhanced the performance of MCN, particularly by applying text cleansing and retrieval augmented generation (RAG) to clinical narratives in German language. Our investigation reveals a notable 6.87 increase in F1 score when using a bi-encoder with a re-ranker, compared to traditional bi- encoder approaches, underscoring ChatGPTs superiority in enhancing MCN tasks. The benefits of document cleansing are especially pro- nounced in scenarios with limited terminological coverage. The impressive ability of ChatGPT to transform raw text into text with more standardized medical terms is an important message, particularly when using MCN for languages with limited support for terminological resources. Nevertheless, our experiments on

German texts reveal the importance of a good terminological basis, even in times of LLMs, when comparing an extract of UMLS Metathesaurus linked to SNOMED codes with a German-specific interface terminol- ogy where F1 values roughly doubled. Looking ahead, adopting LLMs holds promise for streamlining healthcare workflows, reducing documentation errors, and ultimately improving patient care outcomes. Future research should focus on refining these models, expanding their integration into diverse healthcare settings, and evaluating their impact in the real world on clinical practice. Continuous attention must be paid to the settings (e.g., cloud platforms) in which commercial LLMs can be safely used for routine patient data. Likewise, the black-box nature of LLMs with the unpredictability of hallucinations requires careful monitoring and mitigation, e.g., by using additional resources for checking the plausibility of medical term changes proposed by the LLM. Finally, clinical corpora in languages other than English, annotated with codes from standard terminologies at high granularity, are urgently needed as a source of additional ground truth data covering the full spectrum of clinical specialties. This would solve the problems of studies with a limited sample size, such as ours. Nevertheless, we can claim that this study has indicated directions for future research on larger corpora, such as those being created in the ongoing German GeMTeX project 71, which marks an important new step in resource creation for medical concept normalization. AKHILA ABDULNAZAR received the bachelors degree in electronics and biomedical engineering from Cochin University of Science and Technology, India, and the masters degree in applied electronics and instrumentation from Kerala Technological University, India. She is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the Medical University

of Graz, Austria, specializing in standardizing clinical text data using computational semantics, NLP , and LLMs to enhance healthcare interoperability. Prior to her current roles, she was a Software Engineer with Siemens and a Research Intern with Robert Bosch, specializing in AIML applications. Her research interests include data visualization for complex biomedical data empowering healthcare professionals through NLP , LLM, and image classifiers and real-time signal classifiers. ROLAND ROLLER received the degree in computer science and compu- tational linguistics from the University of Trier and Saarland University, and the Ph.D. degree in information extraction from biomedical literature from the University of Sheffield, in 2015. He is a Senior Researcher and the Project Manager with the Speech and Language Technology Group, German Research Center for Artificial Intelligence (DFKI). Currently, he is working on topics related to information extraction, clinical decision support, anonymization, and chatbots. STEFAN SCHULZ is a Full Professor of medical informatics with the Medical University of Graz. He trained as a Medical Doctor with a doctorate in theoretical medicine, he transitioned to medical informatics after two years of clinical practice. An expert in biomedical terminologies and ontologies, he contributes to standards development with SNOMED International. He also holds a part-time position with German NLP Company Averbis. His research group has been pivotal in several EU projects and has hosted international events. With over 250 peer-reviewed publications, he is a highly published researcher and has received multiple awards for his contributions to the field. His research interests include biomedical terminologies, ontologies, electronic health records, and medical language. Corresponding author 979-8-3315-1709-02431.00 2024 IEEE Research and Application of Dialogue Diagnosis and Treatment System Based on Large Language Model Ming Xu Business Development Division, Digital Government Group Taiji Computer Co., Ltd., Beijing, China 113657361qq.com AbstractThe design, research and practical application of conversational diagnosis and treatment system based on Large Language Model (LLM) has potential value in improving outpatient efficiency and reducing medical error rate compared with traditional diagnosis and treatment scenarios. First, the design of the system is based on BERTs (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) architectural idea. GPT-4 (Pre-Trained) model was introduced to generate and expand contexts, and a conversational diagnosis and treatment system based on a large language model was built. The system is pre- trained on a large-scale medical knowledge base, mimics the natural language communication between doctors and patients to carry out in-depth dialogue and interaction, accurately capture the symptoms and health information described by patients, and provide more effective diagnostic suggestions combined with the knowledge of diagnosis and treatment field. Secondly, the doctor-patient dialogue intention recognition algorithm is used to assist the model to capture and analyze the patients emotional expression, medication history and pathological feedback to ensure the accurate transmission and processing of patient information. Finally, when tested in a Tier 3 hospital, the system showed significant advantages over the traditional single-mode dialogue system in three aspects automatic generation of medical record reports, real-time response to patient inquiries, and simulated doctor-patient communication. After more than 1000 doctor-patient conversation tests, the system accurately identified the patients intention ratio reached 95, and the accuracy of diagnostic recommendations obtained a high evaluation of 93. It is expected to become an important auxiliary tool for the medical industry in the future. Keywordslarge language model, conversational diagnostic system, transformer, bidirectional enoceder representations from transformers, generative pre-trained transformer-4 I. INTRODUCTION

With the in-depth application of artificial intelligence technology, especially Large Language Model (LLM) in the field of medical health, conversational diagnosis and treatment system, as an important branch of medical intelligence, has been widely concerned by academia and industry. Over the past decade, dialogue systems have undergone a transformation from simple keyword rules to the complex systems now based on deep learning techniques. Initially, dialogue systems relied on fixed templates and rules formulated by expert experience to respond, which limited the naturalness and flexibility of dialogue. With the wide application of deep learning, especially neural networks in natural language processing1, the generative capability of dialogue systems has been significantly improved. In recent years, the emergence of large language models (LLMS), such as BERT and GPT-4, has revolutionized conversation systems by enabling them to be pre-trained on large-scale data sets to accurately understand and generate natural language in conversation tasks 2,3. This data-driven approach to conversation generation can produce more natural, fluid, diverse, and emotion-rich conversation responses, compensating for the shortcomings of earlier systems. At the same time, the architecture based on Transformer enables LLM to capture long-distance dependencies and understand contextual information, laying a theoretical and technical foundation for building a high-quality conversational diagnosis and treatment system 8. However, when LLM is applied to the design research and practical application in the professional field, it is limited to computing power, large model illusion, fine tuning technology, generalization ability and vector database, etc., and the universality and landing application of large models in the vertical field still have some way to go. With the rapid development of Internet health care, the conversational diagnosis and treatment system needs to consider the timeliness of doctor-patient interaction, the accurate transmission and processing of information, and the high efficiency of the system for modern medical

services. First of all, the dialogue system should be able to respond quickly to the symptoms described by patients, infer the types of diseases according to the symptoms, and provide reasonable medical suggestions. At the same time, it should support cross-platform functions to meet the convenient access of multi-end users and multi-modal doctor-patient dialogue 5. Secondly, through the pre-trained large language model, key information in the diagnosis and treatment dialogue is captured, such as symptom description, medical history review and treatment effect feedback. The dialogue system also needs to be able to continuously update and learn, and fine-adjust the wording and recommendation plan to adapt to individual differences. Combine medical data to realize intelligent medical question and answer analysis. Finally, the construction of the conversational diagnosis and treatment system cannot be separated from the support of medical data. The language model should be built according to the medical knowledge base, and the model should be trained and optimized in combination with doctors diagnosis and treatment experience, so as to achieve the effect of assisting doctors in effective diagnosis and treatment. On this basis, the integrated symptom self-examination, drug inquiry, 1413 2024 8th Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT) 979-8-3315-1709-02431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109ACAIT63902.2024.11021946 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111646 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. appointment registration and other functions will f urther improve the quality and efficiency of outpatient se rvices. In many domestic and foreign literature studies 2,3,5 , there have been cases of conversational diagnosis and tre atment system based on LLM, which adopts deep learning alg orithm and neural network model 6, or BERT model 7-8, self- evolution and self-attention mechanism 9-11, and constantly improves according to user feedback and practical e xperience. This study aims to

explore the application effect a nd optimization strategy of the conversational diagnos is and treatment system based on LLM in the real medical environment, and conduct in-depth research on the m odel performance, adaptability and collaboration with do ctors in the clinical environment, hoping that the systems ability to accurately process professional terms and medical k nowledge can meet the requirements of clinical practice. The implementation of the system integrates diagnosis a nd treatment knowledge and individual needs into the m odel, simulates the communication between doctors and pat ients, and accurately captures symptoms and related health information. An important criterion for measuring t he effectiveness of the system is the comprehensivenes s and accuracy of the conversational system in understand ing the mode of doctor-patient communication, judging the p atients emotions, and providing medical advice. In addition , strategies are proposed to address data privacy and security challenges. This study verifies the core applicatio n value of large language model in conversational diagnosis an d treatment system, and provides technical reference for the development of intelligent medical system in the fu ture. II. T HE POTENTIAL AND CHALLENGES OF LLM FOR DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT In the medical diagnosis and treatment scenario, th e great potential of LLM application in the diagnosis proce ss has attracted wide attention. LLM uses the pre-training of a large number of texts to generate smooth and coherent tex t, which can complete NLP tasks including machine translatio n, text summarization, question answering system and so on. For example, BERT models are able to understand bi-dire ctional context in text using a bi-directional Transformer encoder, further improving the accuracy of language understa nding 1,12. With large-scale parameters and training da ta, LLM can accumulate a wide range of knowledge, showing a high degree of adaptability

and flexibility when communi cating with human language, which improves the ability of the model to handle complex tasks. For example, GPT-3 uses 17 5 billion parameters, significantly enhancing the ability of semantic understanding and logical reasoning 4,13-14. It c an also be used in the research and use of unstructured data s uch as text, language and image in the aspects of information ex traction, knowledge extraction, semantic retrieval and conten t generation 21. The application of LLM in conversa tional diagnosis and treatment systems, such as GPT-4 with a larger parameter level, can accurately capture and respond to patient symptom descriptions by imitating the interaction b etween doctors and patients in processing natural language understanding and generation, so as to achieve effi cient problem induction and rapid extraction of necessary information. At the same time, by using the ability to accurately interpret medical terms acquired through pre- training in the text database in the medical field, and combining the dialogue context to generate a reason able dialogue follow-up 6,12, the language expression of medical professionals can be effectively simulated in the d ialogue 9,15, thus providing a basis for diagnosis. Espec ially in the automatic recording of patient cases, rapid identif ication of symptom information, and estimation of potential di sease risks 10,13, LLM shows the diagnostic ability clo se to that of professional physicians, and can provide basic m edical services to remote areas or resource-poor areas wit h lower cost and wider coverage. In fields such as medical diagnosis, the use of con tent- generating large models to build agents can be used to generate decisions, so as to achieve interaction be tween two worlds the digital world and the real world, which is the so- called general decision large model 17. The lates t large

model design is mainly in the longer context and mu lti-mode, computational efficiency improvement In model trai ning, we mainly explore the use of synthetic data to train l arge models. In terms of reasoning, it mainly reduces the cost o f using models and new algorithms. The application is mainl y on the agent, so the application of enterprise-level large model is becoming more mature 18-19. However, because LLM still has obvious limitations in the depth understanding of medical knowledge and lo gical reasoning, its application in clinical diagnosis mu st be carried out with caution. Taking GPT-4 as an example, previ ous studies have shown that although GPT-4 performs wel l in terms of diagnostic accuracy, giving correct diagno sis results in 64 of cases and placing correct diagnosis in th e first place in 39 of cases 4, its diagnostic process lacks t he display model of knowledge in the medical field and the sym bolic reasoning ability 4,14. In specific medical pract ice, it is still necessary to improve the accuracy and reliability o f medical diagnosis through joint efforts and repeated adjust ments with experts in the field 4. In addition, LLMs lack o f understanding and refinement of the emotions expres sed by patients may lead to misinterpretation of clinical information and misjudgment of the condition. Therefore, in pra ctical applications, the necessity of meticulous evaluatio n by physicians is still irreplaceable. To sum up, the a pplication of LLM in the field of diagnosis and treatment still n eeds to combine medical practice and doctors professional judgment. As an auxiliary tool, LLMs role in diagn ostic support, medical record and preliminary screening c annot be ignored. Meanwhile, its potential and challenges co exist, and it is expected to assume more important responsibil ities

in medically assisted diagnosis and preliminary screen ing in the future 7. III. S YSTEM DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION A. Architecture of conversational diagnosis and treatm ent system based on LLM First of all, the goal of the user layer is to crea te a good user experience, so that users do not feel the exis tence of AI. In the diagnosis and treatment question-and-answer system, it may be a simple dialog box, or it may be integra ted into social products such as WeChat and DingDing, which can be used for text communication, or multimedia methods such as audio or video and images. When users take the init iative to ask questions, it will further stimulate the applic ation layer to ask questions Secondly, the goal of the applicatio n layer is to process the users questions into prompt words s uitable for the model layer the goal of professional prompt wo rds is to adapt the structured abstract parameters of differe nt fields, scenarios, knowledge bases and models, which can be directly used by developers. The prompt words in th is paper are in the vertical field of medical diagnosis. Nex t, we can search our own knowledge base and vector database according to professional prompt words to solve use rs personalized demands and focus on reducing illusion Once again, the contents of the previous two steps are c ombined

into a long prompt word with contextual information Finally, the long prompt words are compressed. The goal of t he model layer is to input the compressed prompt words into the vertical large model of medical diagnosis, use supe rvised learning and reinforcement learning to fine-adjust the changes in the parameters of the large model, gener ate answers after model processing, and then add some n ecessary information according to the users usability and p ersonalized requirements to reply to the user. The model layer is further differentiated into the data layer for model training, deployment, inference, an d illusion reduction. Data layer The data collection and proc essing of constructing a large diagnosis and treatment model mainly involves the collection of a large number of data s ets in the field of medical clinical consultation, including p atient medical records, electronic health records, diagnos tic responses, etc. In addition, there is a need to col lect diverse, high-quality medical inquiry command data in order to equip large models to handle downstream medical diagnosti c tasks. Data construction is a key step to build a specific large- scale language model, including data acquisition, c leaning, labeling and preprocessing. At present, instruction fine- tuning data construction can be automatically const ructed with the help of large language models to save manu al labeling costs. The method of Self-Instruct is main ly used. First, a task seed set is used as the starting poin t of a task pool, multiple task instructions are randomly sampled, an d new instruction contents are generated using large lang uage models. The large language model is used to determi ne whether the instruction task is a classification ta sk (classification task and generation task have diffe rent requirements). Then the large language model is use d

to complete the new instruction content, mainly to gen erate output results, and determine if additional input t ext is needed to generate synchronously Then filter the required data and add the required data to the task pool. At last, re peat the preceding steps until the required number of data i s reached. Model training Typically includes supervised parametric fine-tuning and alignment techniques to adjust the models understanding of medical terms, concepts, a nd contexts to better accommodate consultation needs a nd align with human values 20. In order to avoid consuming a lot of computing power resources in the model fine-tuning and subsequent inference stage, the method of model tun ing adopts the parameter efficient fine-tuning method L oRA, that is, to add additional low-rank matrices to the spec ified parameters (weight matrix) on the large language mo del in parallel, and only train the additional parameters of the parallel low-rank matrix in the model training proc ess. When the rank value is much smaller than the original parameter dimension, the number of new low-rank matrix parame ters is also small. In the downstream task tuning, only sma ll parameters need to be trained, but better performan ce results can be obtained. Model deployment The rapid respon se of the model is critical in medical inquiry applicatio ns, and hardware resource strategies are designed to improv e the reasoning efficiency of the model, improve user exp erience and real-time decision support. Complex reasoning Allows the model to perform multi-step reasoning and decis ion support, which often involves building complex reas oning chains, using scenario simulation and agent decisio n techniques, etc., in order to achieve advanced feat ures such as complex reasoning capabilities for medical consu ltation scenarios. Hallucination reduction Designing large models can effectively deal with hallucination

problems to reduce the risk of misleading decisions, and improve high accu racy and factual requirements in the field of medical consul tation, including the development and application of techni ques to identify and correct the fidelity hallucinations an d factual hallucinations that models may produce when generat ing predictions or explanations. The system design integrates the attention mechanis m proposed in Transformer paper and the powerful feat ure extraction capability of BERT pre-training model 1 1. Meanwhile, semi-supervised learning strategy is ado pted to effectively solve the problem of data scarcity and imbalance in the medical field. In terms of functional module s, the system mainly completes the accurate identification of patients communication intention, such as the rapi d capture and classification of symptom descriptions, and the n guides the subsequent diagnosis process. Through the gener ation capability of the deep learning model GPT-4, the sy stem has the ability to provide personalized medical advice and answer specific patient inquiries in real time. In additio n, the system introduced advanced semantic understanding module t o identify and process the patients emotional expres sion, so as to improve the human level of interaction. The inde pendent doctor-patient dialogue intention recognition algor ithm not only optimizes the accurate transmission of informa tion, but also significantly improves the coherence of multi- round dialogues through the embedded representation of di alogue context. Algorithm implementation and optimization After in-depth analysis of the core algorithm frame work of the conversational diagnosis and treatment syste m based on the large language model, the multi-dimensional strategy is adopted for the system optimization part, and st rive to achieve the double improvement of precision medicin e and user experience. Application architecture of conversational diagnosi s and treatment system with LLM Through careful tuning of Transformer model in medical field, combined with medical professional b ig data and clinical path guide,

the system generates a lan guage model with high medical professionalism. With the s upport of diagnosis and treatment knowledge map, BERTs de ep representation ability is combined with GPT-3s pow erful context generation ability 11, and the language m odel of GPT-3 is used to predict the current word based on the contents of the previous part of the sequence. When predicting the current word, in order to make use o f the contents of the latter part of the current word, th e sequence needs to be arranged and combined. This is the bidi rectional language model that BERT models have, while avoidin g the independence assumptions and symbols of BERT MLM. A t the same time, the two-stream Self-Attention model is introduced, in which the input part includes Two ki nds of training sentences and corresponding position infor mation. The improved transformer is used as feature extract or in structure optimization, including sequence loop mec hanism and relative position coding. On this basis, an int elligent dialogue system with adaptive learning and high-pre cision diagnosis and treatment suggestions is built. The d ialogue management module adopts the method of combining 1416 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111646 UTC from IEEE Xplore. The patient emotion recognition algorithm integrate s natural language processing and machine learning technology , and successfully captures the subtle emotional changes and hidden needs of patients through multi-modal analys is of voice signals and text input. The basis of emotion recognition algorithms

is still the fusion of text and speech f eatures a method that fuses text information and speech featu re information together to improve the expressibility of speech synthesis. The fusion technology can be realized by the following steps encoding the text information as a fixed- length vector, using Word Embedding technology Ext ract speech features using MFCC(Mel-frequency Cepstrum coefficients) Using encoded text information and e xtracted speech feature information as input, the text speec h modes are integrated into LLM by expanding LLM vocabulary . Fine-tuning Data set with voice-to-text cross-modal instruction Text-to-speech mode set, the model is t rained to have good cross-modal instruction following ability and speech-to-speech conversation ability using the ex panded vocabulary training, using text-to-speech data to a lign the two modes, fine-tuning. Emotion recognition is a superv ised learning mechanism that pre-marks similar emotion m arkers for use in training. By comparing a set of text lan guage modes (speech containing medical notes and doctor-patient question-and-answer) with a separate set of text (m edical notes) and a separate set of speech (doctor-patient question- and-answer speech), the multimodal form has a very large advantage in capturing patient emotions. Fine-tuning is based on data sets containing medica l reports and patient cases, among others. In order to make t he model more familiar with medical diagnostic industry term inology and knowledge, supervised fine-tuning is employed, which means that a pre-trained language model is updated with labeled data to complete a specific task, using dat a that has been checked in advance. In this paper, retrieve-au gmented Generation (RAG) is used, which combines natural la nguage generation and information Retrieval to bridge the gap between the extensive knowledge of general models a nd the need for up-to-date knowledge information. RAG, in contrast to fine-tuning, ensures that facts

evolve over time , and that the data embedded in the model architecture is no l onger hard-coded knowledge, allowing for continuous upd ating, removal, or revision of the training data, ensuring that the model remains accurate. The combination of RAG and fine tuning can significantly improve performance. Model data updating corresponds to two typical situations rea l-time updating of medical literature and medical knowledg e base and real-time updating of patients current conditi ons. In the former scenario, more labeled data becomes availabl e after the model is developed and deployed. The latter is a change in the distribution of the data. The former uses an update strategy that blends old models with new models tra ined with old and new data, and uses voting methods to make u se of the latest medical knowledge base The latter scenario takes the old model as the base model, updates the model with old and new data, and uses a stacking approach to ensure th at the patients historical and current diagnoses are avai lable. In addition, attention mechanisms and anti-noise encoders are introduced to enhance the models judg ment of drug side effects and case specificity, improve the accuracy of information in communication and the rationality of medical decisions. The system has been shown to be able to reach conclusions comparable to expert-level clinic al diagnoses in multiple rounds of conversations, and has demonstrated excellent stability and reliability wh en processing real patient data. The system integrates analysis of clinical medication history and test results, and u ses deep neural networks for data mining and pattern recogni tion to help identify early diseases and recommend preventi ve measures. The real-time updated medical literature and guidelines are included in the learning scope, so t hat the system can absorb the latest

medical information, a nd in the iterative evolution of algorithms, medical errors a re reduced and diagnosis and treatment efficiency is improved. While ensuring the accuracy of information processing, th e system makes customized adjustments for user experience, a nd through continuous optimization of semantic underst anding and generation algorithms, the doctor-patient dialo gue becomes more intelligent and intimate. C. Data processing and Security Privacy and System Testing The basic requirements of generative AI service sec urity and personal privacy cover training data security requirements, model security requirements and perso nal information protection, so as to strengthen the sec urity of the development and application of generative AI. It is divided into data source security, data content security an d model security requirements. Data source security includes 1) Management of collection sources. The training data of this syste m come from the case records of certain diagnosis and trea tment of three hospitals and the diagnosis and treatment dia logue between doctors and patients, etc., which can ensur e that the illegal and bad information in the data content is very low or none 2) Training data includes case records and di agnosis and treatment data of various types of diseases, in cluding data sources, text, pictures, audio and video. 3) The tr aining data comes from the authorization of the partner hospita l and is traceable. Data content security includes 1) content filterin g of the data, specifying some illegal and bad information k eywords, classifying the data according to clinical medical specialties, and appropriate manual sampling processing. 2) Iden tify content related to intellectual property rights bef ore the use of training data, and negotiate the ownership of in tellectual property rights of case record data. 3) Filter sens itive information, including personal information and key characteristics of

both doctors and patients, and r etain non- sensitive information for training, such as patient number, gender, age, etc. Model security requirements include 1) In terms of model training, the content security of model gener ation is taken as the main index to evaluate the quality of the generated results, and vulnerability scanning and identification of the system framework are carried out regularly. 2) In terms of model output, technical m easures such as manual inspection and automatic recognition of 1417 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. IV. C ONCLUSIONS After long-term comprehensive research and large-sc ale clinical experiments, the conversational diagnosis and treatment system based on the large language model proposed in this paper has shown obvious advantages in impro ving the efficiency and accuracy of diagnosis and treatment. The system collects and pre-trains the knowledge base c overing

the medical corpus through BERT architecture, which ensures that the model can accurately understand th e professional terms in the medical field, while stre ngthening the context generation and expansion ability with G PT-4 model to achieve in-depth doctor-patient dialogue interaction. The test results show that the system has achieved 95 accuracy in capturing patient intent and 93 ac curacy in diagnostic recommendations, which is far superio r to similar single-mode dialogue systems. The empirical analysis of doctor-patient conversation revealed that the sy stem not only identified symptom descriptions and related he alth information, but also effectively identified patien ts emotional expression, providing a consultation expe rience close to human doctors. In addition, the applicatio n of the system significantly improves the outpatient effici ency, potentially reduces the doctors work burden, reduc es the medical error rate, and shows a broad application p rospect in the medical and health field. In this study, advanc ed programming skills and advanced algorithm design principles are adopted to overcome many technical difficulties in big data processing and natural lan guage processing in the algorithm implementation and opti mization of conversational diagnosis and treatment system. T he designed doctor-patient dialogue intention recognit ion algorithm is especially aimed at the complexity and diversity of the medical field, and a large number of case da ta are trained by machine learning method to achieve high- precision intention recognition. The complex module s and multi-level data analysis of the system integration ensure a deep understanding and precise processing of medica l conversations, which greatly promotes the developme nt of medical artificial intelligence. Finally, the resea rch of this system has not only made breakthroughs in technolog y and efficiency, but also made important contributions i n improving patient experience, which has a positive impact on promoting the

Reducing Diagnostic Uncertainty in Emergency Departments The Role of Large Language Models in Age-Specific Diagnostics Wanting Cui dept. of Biomedical Informatics University of Utah Salt Lake City, UT, USA u6048974utah.edu Kensaku Kawamoto dept. of Biomedical Informatics University of Utah Salt Lake City, UT, USA kensaku.kawamotoutah.edu Keaton Morgan dept. of Biomedical Informatics University of Utah Salt Lake City, UT, USA keaton.morganhsc.utah.edu Joseph Finkelstein dept. of Biomedical Informatics University of Utah Salt Lake City, UT, USA Joseph.Finkelsteinutah.edu AbstractDiagnostic errors in emergency departments affect approximately 7.4 million patients every year. To address this, the integration of artificial intelligence, specifically Large Language Models (LLMs) like ClinicalBERT, into differential diagnostic process has been explored to reduce the diagnostic uncertainty and alleviate physician workload. This study focused on assessing the variance in diagnostic accuracy of LLMs between young and middle-aged adults (18-64 years) and older adults (65 years) using 13 models fine-tuned on the MIMIC-III dataset, each targeting a specific body system. There were 8,321 cases of hospital stays and 124,736 clinical notes in the analytic dataset. Results indicated that while some mode ls performed consistently well across both age groups, there was a discernible variability in others. 77 of models showed strong performance for younger adults, compared to 54 for older adults. The neoplasm (NEO) and circulatory (CIR) models stood out in both groups. In addition, the respiratory systems showed better performance for older adults. The mental and behavioral (MBD) system models, however, demonstrated a significant decline in recall for older adults. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating age-specific adjustments into AI models to optimize diagnostic precision across diverse patient populations. Keywords Large language models, diagnostic uncertainty, emergency department I. INTRODUCTION Emergency medicine demands both precision and rapidity in decision-making, as the outcomes can be critical for patients presenting with a wide array of

symptoms. Within this fast- paced environment, the emergency department (ED) staff are tasked with identifying the underlying causes of a patients distress rapidly and accurately to administer the appropriate care. However, despite advancements in medical knowledge and diagnostics, emergency departments still face significant challenges related to diagnostic errors. Recent studies have showed that out of the 130 million emergency department visits in the United States annually, approximately 7.4 million may result in misdiagnoses 1. When a patient enters the ED, the symptoms presented can often be vague, overlapping, and not easily attributable to a single cause without thorough investigation 2-4. The task is further complicated by various cognitive biases, where the first impressions or most apparent symptoms can unduly influence the clinicians judgment 5-6. Given the complex nature of these challenges, strategies to improve diagnostic accuracy are crucial. Incorporating artificial intelligence (AI) into the diagnostic process has emerged as a solution in recent years. AI, particularly in the form of Large Language Models (LLMs), has shown promise in expanding differential diagnosis and reducing cognitive load on physicians 7-10. By training AI algorithms with clinical notes, the potential of LLMs was explored in a pilot project assessing feasibility in identifying the organ system responsible for acute admissions in hospitals by fine-tuning a ClinicalBERT model using 3 days of clinical notes from the MIMIC-III (Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care III) dataset 11. ClinicalBERT is a modification of the original BERT model that has been further trained on patient clinical notes using the MIMIC III dataset 12. MIMIC III is a comprehensive database from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston. It contains extensive health records from the hospital useful for such AI applications 13. In addition, in another pilot study, high diagnostic uncertainty was associated with older adults with multiple number

of comorbidities14. Thus, the goal of this study is to evaluate how these models diagnostic accuracy varies across different age demographics, particularly between young and middle-aged adults (18-64 years) and older adults (65 years). This will help in understanding the necessity and effectiveness of age-specific AI diagnostic models. II. METHOD In the prior pilot study, 19 distinct models were fine-tuned using the MIMIC-III dataset to determine the diagnostic body system11. The foundational model was ClinicalBERT, developed by Huang et al. (2020), which was built upon Googles BERT model 12. The pilot study was aimed at predicting the body system associated with a patients discharge diagnosis based on clinical notes from the initial three days of hospital admission. Nineteen classification models were created, each targeting a specific body system. Each models fine-tuning 525 2024 IEEE 12th International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI) 2575-26342431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109ICHI61247.2024.00076 2024 IEEE 12th International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI) 979-8-3503-8373-72431.00 2024 IEEE DOI 10.1109ICHI61247.2024.00076 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111854 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. involved a single training epoch based on the ClinicalBERT model, with 80 of the data constituting the training set. This study aimed to further assess these fine-tuned models trained in the pilot study. Multiple test sets were created, using 10 of the remaining data. The inclusion criteria were patients who had clinical notes and discharge diagnosis were included. In addition, only adult patients who were admitted through the emergency department were included. For exclusion criteria, children and those cases without clinical notes or valid discharge diagnoses were excluded. Patients were further divided into two age groups young adults, which included patients between 18 and 64 years old, and older adults, which included patients who were 65 years or older at

time of admission. In addition, only notes from the first 24 hours of admission were used in the test sets. Focus was placed on 13 models, as some originally did not exhibit optimal performance, and others had a very limited test size. Similar to previous study, discharge diagnoses were categorized using the Clinical Classifications Software Refined (CCSR) 15. Body systems were then identified using the first 3 characters of the category code. Given that multiple notes could be generated per patient stay, texts from these notes were concatenated into a single document. Thes texts were then processed to eliminate special characters and to expand abbreviations. The maximum input size for the ClinicalBERT model in use was limited to 512 tokens. The model uses sub- word tokenization, which breaks down words into components like prefixes, stems, and postfixes, leading to an increased number of tokens. To manage this, the concatenated text was divided into segments of 318 words each. Each word segment was then processed through tokenization, which included adding CLS and SEP tokens and converting them into token IDs. The performance of the system was evaluated by applying the model to each test item. Since multiple text segments were included for each stay, and they may predict different results, the final label was determined using the probability metric established by Huang et al. (2020). The evaluation of each distinct model was conducted using metrics such as precision, recall, F1 score, and accuracy. III. RESULT There were 8,321 cases of hospital stays and 124,736 pieces of notes in the analytic dataset. 4,173 stays were young and middle-aged adults, and 4,148 stays were older adults. In older adults, there were a similar amount of female and male patients. 2,059 (49.64) patients were female, and 2,089 (50.36) patients were male. However, in the

young and middle-aged group, there were significantly higher proportions of male patients than female patien ts. 2,529 (60.60) were male patients, compared to 1, 644 (39.40) female patients. Older adult patients were associated with 6.172.23 diagnostic body systems per hospital stay, and young and middle-aged patients were associated with 5.362.48 diagnostic body systems per hospital stay. The most diagnosed body systems for young and middle-aged adults were circulatory system (CIR) (n 6,112), injury (INJ) (n 5,245), and endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease (END) (n 5,065). In contrast, the most diagnosed body systems for older adults were circulatory system (CIR) (n 12,464), endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease (END) (n 6,277), and genitourinary system (GEN) (n 4,995). There were significantly higher proportions of diseases of the circulatory system (CIR) in older adults, constituting 28.11 of cases, compared to 16.28 in young and middle-aged adults. In addition, diseases of the genitourinary system (GEN) were significantly more common in older adults, constituting 11.26 of cases, compared to 7.52 in young and middle-aged adults. Table 1 provided an overview of the performance of 13 diagnostic models, with each system evaluated in two distinct age groups. The models were assessed on four metrics precision, recall, F1 score, and accuracy. For the young and middle-aged group, the highest precision was in the injury and poisoning (INJ) model at 0.92, followed by neoplasm (NEO) and digestive system, which suggested these models were very accurate when they identified cases as positive. The highest recall was in the circulatory (CIR) system and endocrine (END) system models at 0.81, indicating they were good at identifying most actual positive cases. The F1 score, which balanced precision and recall, was also highest for injury (INJ) and neoplasm (NEO) at 0.82. The neoplasm model had higher accuracy than the injury model. In the older age group, the circulatory (CIR) model performed well in recall at 0.98, showing that it rarely misses positive cases. However, the

mental and behavioral (MBD) system model showed a significant drop in recall to 0.36, suggesting it often missed true positive cases in older adults. The F1 score was highest for circulatory model at 0.88, indicating a strong balance between precision and recall. In addition, accuracy was highest for the circulatory and the nervous system models at 0.81. IV. DISCUSSION In the comparative analysis of diagnostic models, 77 of them had strong performance for young adults, surpassing a threshold of 0.7 across all evaluated metrics. However, only 54 of the models achieved similar results in the older adult group, which suggested better performance metrics for younger patients. Notably, the neoplasm (NEO) and circulatory (CIR) models stood out in both groups, which demonstrated a well- balanced trade-off between precision and recall as reflected in their robust F1 scores and accuracy. The variability in model performance across age groups was a critical finding, which highlighted the necessity of age-specific approaches in the development and deployment of LLMs in clinical settings. Such differences may be attributed to the divergence in clinical manifestations of diseases in different age cohorts or the variance in the quantity and quality of clinical notes available for model training. Furthermore, the discrepancies in performance metrics like recall for the MBD model between age groups tested the models generalizability. It also suggested a need for age-tailored models or the incorporation of age as a variable within the models to improve their diagnostic accuracy. V. CONCLUSION The performance of the models varied across age categories, with young and middle-aged adult groups generally achieving better outcomes compared to the older adult group. Nonetheless, the models specific to the circulatory (CIR) and respiratory (RSP) systems showed higher performance scores in the older adults cohort. Additionally, the models for neoplasm (NEO) and circulatory (CIR) systems

exhibited strong F1 scores and high accuracy in both demographic groups. While some of the models showed good results, the variations in model performance between different age groups indicated that demographic factors like age should be taken into consideration during the development of these models. This approach could potentially mitigate the risk of diagnostic errors and improve the overall quality of healthcare delivery. Future research should aim to understand the underlying reasons for performance disparities and explore the integration of additional demographic and clinical variables to refine the precision and utility of LLMs in medical diagnostics REFERENCES 1 Newman-Toker DE, Peterson SM, Badihian S, et al. Enhancing Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support by Incorporating Clinical Practice Guidelines David Oniani University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA davidonianipitt.edu Xizhi Wu University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA xiw183pitt.edu Shyam Visweswaran University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA shv3pitt.edu Sumit Kapoor University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA suk158pitt.edu Shravan Kooragayalu UPMC Western Maryland Cumberland, MD, USA knsk86gmail.com Katelyn Polanska University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA kate.tikhpitt.edu Y anshan Wang University of Pittsburgh Pittsburgh, PA, USA yanshan.wangpitt.edu AbstractLarge Language Models (LLMs), enhanced with Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs), can signiﬁcantly improve Clinical Decision Support (CDS). However, approaches for incorporating CPGs into LLMs are not well studied. In this study, we develop three distinct methods for incorporating CPGs into LLMs Binary Decision Tree (BDT), Program-Aided Graph Construction (PAGC), and Chain-of-Thought-Few-Shot Prompting (CoT-FSP), and focus on CDS for COVID-19 outpatient treatment as the case study. Zero-Shot Prompting (ZSP) is our baseline method. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed methods, we create a set of synthetic patient descriptions and conduct both automatic and human evaluation of the responses generated by four LLMs GPT-4, GPT-3.5 Turbo, LLaMA, and PaLM 2. All four LLMs exhibit improved performance when enhanced with CPGs compared to the baseline ZSP . BDT outperformed both CoT-FSP and PAGC in automatic evaluation. All of the proposed methods demonstrate high performance in human evaluation. LLMs enhanced with CPGs outperform plain LLMs with ZSP in providing accurate recommendations for COVID-19 outpatient treatment, highlighting the potential for broader applications beyond the case study. Index Termsartiﬁcial intelligence, large language models, clinical decision support, prompting, generative ai I. I NTRODUCTION Large Language Models (LLMs)1 have opened new op- portunities in many ﬁelds, with applications ranging from creative writing 1 to chemical research 2. LLMs have also been studied in the context of healthcare, where they have

proven successful at numerous tasks, including clinical reasoning 3, question-answering 4, and medical evidence summarization 5, among other applications. Moreover, re- cent research produced powerful healthcare LLMs, such as GatorTron 6 and Med-PaLM 7. Equal contributionCorresponding author 1Note that in this paper, the term LLM refers to a generative Large Language Model (LLM). The success and popularity of LLMs are at least partially due to their In-Context Learning (ICL) capability 8. ICL is a learning method that does not require model parameter updates but learns from the given context. It can be thought of as a higher-order function that takes several few-shot examples (i.e., the context) to produce the predictor function 9. The emergence of ICL-capable LLMs has provided an alternative to a typical pre-train, ﬁne-tune, and predict pipeline, with prompting replacing ﬁne-tuning. Prompting is a novel paradigm where, with the use of textual prompts, downstream tasks are modeled as those typically solved during pre-training 10. It has given rise to Prompt Engineering (PE) 11, a ﬁeld that aims to design prompts that enhance ICL and improve LLM reasoning. One of several widely used PE methods 12 14 is Chain-of-Thought (CoT) 15 prompting, which attempts to improve LLM reasoning by providing intermediate reasoning steps as part of the prompt. Clinical Decision Support (CDS) supports healthcare decision-making by enabling the timely delivery of evidence- based guidelines at the point of care 16, 17. Optimizing CDS has the potential to signiﬁcantly improve clinical workﬂows, and it has been one of the major topics of discussion in healthcare 18. The importance of CDS, paired with its recent focus on patient-centered solutions 19, 20, demands novel methods for improving the quality of healthcare decision- making, ultimately facilitating better health outcomes. At the same time, the literature on LLM-driven algorithmic methods that incorporate CPGs is

limited. LLMs offer many beneﬁts for CDS, such as timely decision support, ease of use due to their interactive nature, and the ability to capture both Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs) and patient-speciﬁc information. In this paper, we propose three methods for improving LLMs for CDS by incorporating CPGs Binary Decision Tree (BDT), Program-Aided Graph Construction (PAGC), and Chain- of-Thought-Few-Shot Prompting (CoT-FSP). The evaluation was done by two physicians from the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center (UPMC). We used Zero-Shot Prompting (ZSP) as the baseline. The experimental results show that the proposed methods provide signiﬁcant improvements over the baseline, which speaks to the effectiveness of the methods. A. Related Work The ﬁeld of using LLMs for CDS is rapidly evolving. Here we summarized

related work up to the point when this paper was drafted. A recent study considered ChatGPT 21 LLM for improving CDS 22, reaching the conclusion that LLMs can be important complementary parts for optimizing CDS. In another study, researchers examined LLMs for improving CDS in personalized oncology 23, reaching a similar conclusion that LLMs did not achieve the quality and credibility of human experts, but generated helpful responses that could complement established procedures. There have also been studies evaluating LLMs for CDS in speciﬁc ﬁelds, such as cardiology 24 and orthopedics 25. Other studies 26, 27 considered methods like Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) 28 to facilitate CDS. It should be noted that none of the studies augmented LLMs with CPGs, nor did they consider advanced prompting methods such as the ones proposed in our study. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the ﬁrst one to develop and evaluate such methods. II. M ETHODS We propose three methods enhanced by CPGs as well as a baseline Zero-Shot Prompting (ZSP) method. Figure 1 illustrates the methods. A. COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines We focused on supporting outpatient treatment decisions for Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19). Because COVID- 19 was a novel disease at the start of the pandemic, clini- cal practice guidelines evolved rapidly in response to new discoveries and treatments. Keeping up with the frequent guideline updates was difﬁcult for healthcare providers. In such a situation, a CDS tool like LLM can be valuable in providing reliable support for decision making in clinical care. For the COVID-19 outpatient treatment guidelines, we used the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) COVID-19 Outpatient Treatment Guidelines 29. The CPGs provide a comprehensive step-by-step approach that outlines outpatient treatment options. We slightly revised the guidelines to reﬂect insights

and recommendations from our physicians. Body weight, in particular, was used as a key determinant for COVID- 19 treatment since many medication dosages are weight-based. We considered the patients underlying medical conditions, COVID-19 related symptoms, and the duration of symptoms to determine if they were in the high-risk versus low-risk category. Based on this information, the guidelines offer three outpatient treatment options for high-risk patientsPaxlovid, Remdesivir, 695 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. The revised COVID-19 Outpatient Treatment Guidelines were used to build LLM-enhanced CDS systems for COVID-19 outpatient treatment decision support and can be found in the Appendix. B. Binary Decision Tree (BDT) Binary Decision Tree (BDT) is a recursive algorithm guided by CPGs. We model CPGs as a binary tree and use an LLM to navigate it. There are two calls to the LLM. In the ﬁrst call, an input to an LLM contains a description of the task, several few-shot examples, and a question from CPGs (i.e., node value). The

generated output is stored. In the second call, we use the response from the ﬁrst call to ask a yes-no question of whether the answer was afﬁrmative or negative. Depending on the generated response, we recurse to the left or right subtree till reaching one of the leaf nodes. Algorithm 1 is the pseudocode for the method. C. Chain-of-Thought-Few-Shot Prompting (CoT-FSP) In this method, we use a prompt to describe the CPGs. Speciﬁcally, we construct a prompt containing a description of the task, ﬁve few-shot examples, and an if-else description of CPGs. The if-else description is an algorithmic seven-step CoT prompt. At every step, CoT-FSP decides between selecting a speciﬁc treatment and progressing to the next step. The selection of a speciﬁc treatment ends the chain. In the ﬁrst step, the algorithm checks whether the patient has COVID- 19, and if they do, it moves to the second step. Otherwise, it provides the treatment suggestion V accination and booster is recommended. In the second step, the algorithm checks whether the patient needs hospitalization or increased oxygen. In the third step, the algorithm looks for risk factors for severe COVID-19. If the hypothetical patient has risk factors, it goes to the fourth, ﬁfth, and seventh steps to ﬁnd an appropriate treatment such as Paxlovid, Remdesivir,o r Molnupiravir.I f none of the treatment options are available for the patient, it will suggest the patient get monitoring and supportive care and end the process. The ﬁve few-shot examples are ﬁve hypothetical patient descriptions and ﬁve answers on providing treatment sug- gestions after going through the step-by-step if-else process. We carefully selected more complex patient cases to improve ICL and handle challenging scenarios. Algorithm2 shows the pseudocode for the method. Zero-Shot Prompting (ZSP) Zero-Shot Prompting (ZSP) method constructs a prompt that contains a task description and patient description, followed by a query. In contrast with BDT, CoT-FSP , and PAGC methods, ZSP does not utilize CPGs or few-shot examples. Instead, ZSP produces the output directly from a patient description. Synthetic Patient Dataset For rigorous

evaluation of the proposed methods, we created a synthetic patient dataset based on CPGs. We modeled CPGs as a binary tree with eight leaf nodes. There were thirteen different paths to reach one of the leaves. Considering three different difﬁculty categories (easy, medium, and hard), we created 39 synthetic patients to examine all possible paths. The descriptions for each difﬁculty category are listed in the Appendix. G. Evaluation The evaluation has two stages best method selection and human annotation. We considered four LLMs, GPT-4 31, GPT-3.5 Turbo 32, LLaMA 33, and PaLM 2 34, for a more holistic evaluation. In the method selection stage, we automatically evaluate the approaches and select those with an F-score greater than 0.5. Systematically evaluating few-shot classiﬁcation performance is challenging as predictions on small datasets can be unstable 35. To ensure the reproducibility and robustness of results, we performed four runs with random seeds (9631, 4603, 6367, and 4057) and computed the mean for each metric3. The human evaluation has two rounds a round for ensuring agreement among two UPMC physicians (authors S.K. and S.K.) and the ﬁnal LLM response annotation round 4.W e used random seed 8747 for generating the responses. In the ﬁrst round, the annotators rated ten questions for evaluation. We used Gwets AC1 for computing Inter-Annotator Agree- ment (IAA) 36. For interpreting IAA scores, we used Landis and Koch scale 37. T ABLE I Gwets AC1 scores for three different questions asked to physicians in the ﬁrst round. III. R ESULTS We ﬁrst performed the automatic evaluation and then picked the seven most performant methods for the human evaluation stage, namely GPT-4 BDT, GPT-4 CoT-FSP, GPT-3.5 Turbo BDT, GPT-4 PAGC, PaLM 2 BDT , GPT- 3.5 Turbo CoT-FSP, and PaLM 2 CoT-FSP. Pairing our methods with LLaMA-13b did not yield promis- ing results. As a result, the model was excluded entirely from the human evaluation stage. In its defense, however, LLaMA- 13b is signiﬁcantly smaller than the other models, and a larger LLaMA model could perform better. ZSP performed poorly across all models, and we also excluded it due to this reason. Table II shows and ranks the selected methods. In the human evaluation stage, two UPMC physicians (authors S.K. and S.K.) annotated 49 responses generated using the proposed methods. We generated seven responses using every method and computed the mean score for each evaluation category. TableIII shows the human evaluation results. GPT-4 CoT-FSP , GPT-4 PAGC, and

PaLM 2 BDT obtained the perfect scores, with the highest quality of responses (rating of 2) for all queries. GPT-3.5 Turbo BDT was next, with an overall average of 1.86. PaLM 2 CoT-FSP , GPT-3.5 Turbo CoT- FSP , and GPT-4 BDT showed the worst performance, with average scores of 1.43, 1.24, and 1.10, respectively. We should note that in all cases, the average score was above 1.00, which speaks to the promising performance of the approaches. Interestingly, the best method in the automatic evaluation, GPT-4 BDT, did not perform as well in human evaluation. At the same time, among models that utilized BDT, PaLM 2 and GPT-3.5 Turbo demonstrated strong performance across all queries. IV . DISCUSSION Aiming to improve CDS, we proposed three new methods for incorporating CPGs into LLMs. We took COVID-19 CDS 5Note that 0.61 would have already been a substantial agreement, but we obtained 0.60, which was 0.01 less. as the case study and created a set of synthetic patients. We ﬁrst selected methods with an F-score higher than 0.5 and then performed a rigorous human evaluation of the approaches. The proposed methods outperformed the baseline ZSP and have shown high performance across the tasks. Our work also opens future directions of research. The promising performance of the proposed methods allows for incorporating them as part of software systems, such as chatbots, that streamline CDS. As part of this work, we have also built a prototype, shown in Figure 2. The system takes the prompt from a medical professional as input and provides treatment recommendations, enhancing the clinical pipeline and facilitating accurate healthcare delivery. A case study exploring systems utilizing the proposed methods in real-world clinical settings is another interesting future direction of research. Furthermore, the generalizability of the methods allows for their use in

any case study or ﬁeld (even beyond healthcare), where guidelines are present and formulated as a tree. Future research can also focus on improving the proposed methods. An example of such improvement could be tree pruning or node skipping in the case of BDT. In other words, some responses may contain enough information to answer several questions, allowing for shortcuts across the tree. Proposing completely different LLM-based approaches for enhancing CDS could be another avenue for exploration. In addition, we would like to emphasize the need for transparent and privacy-aware development of LLMs and related methods, which is especially important in the case of healthcare 38, where even a small-scale accidental leak of Protected Health Information (PHI) can be dangerous. Open- source models offer transparency, allowing researchers to audit the codebase if necessary. On the other hand, proprietary LLMs typically offer the Application Programming Interface (API) model, where the codebase is not publicly available, and there is no clarity on where the user or user request data is kept (and for how long). Among the four models used in our study, only LLaMA6 was open-source. Finally, we also note that experiments with LLMs can be costly. In our case, the associated costs for conducting experi- ments with GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 Turbo were approximately 500 6httpsgithub.comfacebookresearchllama 698 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Available httpsdoi.org10.1038s41746-023-00965-x APPENDIX Human Evaluation Guidelines The presence of incorrect medical content category aims to discern the accuracy of the generated answers concerning medical content. The objective is to determine whether the responses contain information conﬂicting with established medical guidance. The omission of content evaluates the completeness of the generated answers and aims to identify instances where essential information

is either inadequately addressed or omitted entirely. Understanding the models ability to provide comprehensive responses is crucial for its practical utility in clinical decision-making. Thepresence of possible harmful content is an essential consideration in our evaluation framework as the identiﬁcation of potential harm that LLMs could pose to users relying on the generated answers. This category is responsible for ensuring the safety and ethical use of the methods in real-world healthcare applications, particularly in sensitive domains such as healthcare. Three human evaluation categories Presence of Incorrect Medical Content 0) Y es, great clinical signiﬁcance (there is incorrect medical content and it will make great clinical signiﬁcance on the treatment recommendation, such as an incorrect treatment suggestions based on patients conditions, like recommending patient who does not wish to go to hospital for Remdesivir infusion treatment) 1) Y es, little clinical signiﬁcance (there is incorrect medical content and it will make little clinical signiﬁcance on the treatment recommendation) 2) No (i.e., no incorrect medical content) Omission of Content 0) Y es, great clinical signiﬁcance (there is omission of the input patients condition or characteristics and it will make great clinical signiﬁcance on the treatment recommendation, such as changing the result of recommendation) 1) Y es, little clinical signiﬁcance (there is omission of the patients condition or characteristics and it will make little clinical signiﬁcance on the treatment recommendation, for example, even though omission of content occurred, it does not change the ultimate result) 2) No (i.e., no omission of content) Presence of Possible Harmful Content 0) Death or severe harm 1) Moderate or mild harm 2) No harm (i.e., no harmful content) Synthetic Patient Difﬁculty Categories In the easy category, patient descriptions adhered closely to the phrases used in the COVID-19 CGPs. Such an approach allowed for assessing methods in making

correct decisions at each checkpoint and identifying the correct treatment path following the guidelines. Themedium category contained pa- tient descriptions that used synonyms and phrases semantically equivalent to those in the CPGs. For example, instead of describing a patient as one who had a positive COVID-19 test, the description mentioned a positive Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) test. The goal was to evaluate the ability to comprehend the semantic meaning in the patient description, correctly map it to the relevant checkpoint, and make the correct decision. The hard category included patient descriptions that differed considerably from the phrases used in the CPGs. We also intentionally included unrelated and subtle information. Hence, the methods would not only need to extract symptoms from the description but also ﬁlter out irrelevant information.
