HepatoAudit A Comprehensive Dataset for Evaluating Consistency of Large Language Models in Hepatobiliary Case Record Diagnosis HanZhi Zhang Lab. of AI, Hangzhou Institute of Technology of Xidian University Xidian University Hangzhou, China 22171214767stu.xidian.edu.cn Fangchen Dong School of Communications and Information Engineering North University of China Taiyuan,China dongf343gmail.com Weibin Li Lab. of AI, Hangzhou Institute of Technology of Xidian University Xidian University Hangzhou, China weibinlixidian.edu.cn Corresponding author. Yi Ren Lab. of AI, Hangzhou Institute of Technology of Xidian University Xidian University Hangzhou, China 22171214767stu.xidian.edu Hao Dong Department of Hepatobiliary Surgery II Central Hospital of Xianyang Xianyang , China 271586416qq.com AbstractIn recent years, large language models (LLMs) have shown significant potential in the medical field. However, most existing research focuses on general medical test sets, with relatively insufficient exploration in real clinical applications, especially in the field of complex diseases. To fill this g ap, we introduce the HepatoAudit dataset the first comprehensive dataset designed to evaluate the consistency of LLMs in generating diagnostic records for hepatobiliary diseases. This dataset is collected from the Chinese Clinical Cases Outcome Database and contains complete medical records that have undergone peer review, including patient basic information, chief complaints, history of present illness, personal history, family history, past medical history, physical examination, and laboratory tests. We have carefully selected 20 common hepatobiliary diseases, totaling 684 medical records. We systematically evaluated six mainstream large language models using four prompting methods. The experimental results show that although all models perform high in precision (with GPT-4 reaching a maximum of 93.42), there are still significant deficiencies in recall, with the recall rate generally below 70. In particular, for diseases with atypical symptoms or caused by multiple factors, such as splenomegaly, fatty liver, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes, the accuracy of the models is even below 40.The main reasons are

the insufficient understanding of the patients complete medical history and symptoms by the large language models, as well as the lack of effective reflection and error correction mechanisms. Our research not only points out the challenges of LLMs in professional medical diagnostic tasks but also provides important directions for future improvements. KeywordsLarge Language Models,Hepatobiliary Case Dataset, Large Language Models Evaluation,Medical Case Record Generation I. INTRODUCTION Hepatobiliary diseases encompass a variety of conditions ranging from cirrhosis and cholangitis to liver cancer, with diverse causes and manifestations. These diseases often present clinical symptoms similar to other conditions, making diagnosis particularly complex in severe cases. Given their complex pathological features, clinical diagnosis faces high uncertainty and misdiagnosis rates. In recent years, with advancements in medical technology, researchers have increasingly explored the use of machine learning and deep learning techniques for diagnosing hepatobiliary diseases. However, these technologies encounter significant limitations in real -world diagnostic applications, mainly due to insufficient logical reasoning abilities, poor model interpretability, and a reliance on large - scale, high -quality annotated data. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have achieved significant breakthroughs. Models like ChatGPT 1 and GPT -42 possess extensive knowledge and logical reasoning capabilities, allowing them to effectively handle and understand a wide range of complex language tasks. Due to their strong generalization and multi-task learning abilities, researchers have begun explor ing their applications across multiple specialized fields and have achieved some promising results3,4,5. In the medical field, Jin et al. developed MedQA 6, the first multiple-choice dataset to assess the medical capabilities of LLMs, while Xidong Wang and colleagues introduced CMB7 for multi-level evaluation of LLMs knowledge in Chinese medicine. Although numerous studies have evaluated the applications of LLMs in specialized fields, there remains a lack of datasets designed to assess complex clinical and real- world application sc

enarios. To address this gap, we designed the HepatoAudit dataset, focusing on real clinical cases in hepatology to evaluate the performance of LLMs in generating consistent diagnostic records in hepatobiliary cases. The HepatoAudit dataset comprises medical records of 684 real patients, covering 20 complex hepatobiliary clinical scenarios, including diseases like cirrhosis, cholangitis, and hepatic space -occupying lesions. Each record contains detailed chief complaints, history of present illness, past medical history, and laboratory results, based on real 234 2025 IEEE 17th International Conference on Computer Research and Development 979-8-3315-3188-12531.00 2025 IEEE 2025 IEEE 17th International Conference on Computer Research and Development (ICCRD) 979-8-3315-3188-12531.00 2025 IEEE DOI 10.1109ICCRD64588.2025.10963108 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on June 25,2025 at 111208 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. clinical diagnoses, guiding LLMs to generate professional and compliant diagnostic records. Using the HepatoAudit dataset, we evaluated several current advanced LLMs. Experimental results indicate that even within this specialized diagnostic context, LLMs showed substantial performance gaps, with recall rates generally not exceeding 70. This fin ding demonstrates that, despite their strong language comprehension and knowledge reasoning capabilities, current LLMs still struggle to accurately simulate physicians diagnostic reasoning processes in complex clinical scenarios involving hepatobiliary di seases, revealing significant limitations in reasoning consistency and decision support. The main contributions of this study are as follows 1) Introduction of the HepatoAudit dataset This is the first comprehensive dataset focused on evaluating the consistency of LLMs in generating diagnostic records for hepatobiliary cases. 2) Revealing the limitations of current LLMs in professional medical diagnostic settings Experimental results show that LLMs cannot yet meet clinical standards for accuracy and consistency in diagnosing complex hepatobiliary conditions. 3) Providing a benchmark for future research on LLMs in medicine HepatoAudit can serve as a reference and benchmark for future research on

LLMs in the medical field, promoting improvements in model diagnostic capabilities in specific disease areas. II. PROPOSED METHOD A. Creation of the Evaluation Dataset The HepatoAudit dataset is derived from the Chinese Clinical Case Achievement Database, a national-level platform for publishing clinical case achievements, funded by the China Association for Science and Technology and hosted by the Chinese Medical Associ ation Journal. First, we used web - scraping technology to extract a large number of clinical case texts from this database. The web scraper followed the websites Robots protocol to ensure legal and compliant data collection. Next, we collected hepatology-specific professional terms and keywords and utilized topic models such as LDA8 to identify hepatology-related thematic texts. To ensure data accuracy and diversity, we applied hash functions and text similarity algorithms to remove duplicate and similar content. To protect patient privacy, we employed automated data anonymization techniques. Specifically, we used named entity recognition (NER)9 algorithms to automatically detect and remove personally identifiable information, such as names, ID numbers, and contact details, from the text. Additionally, we applied rule -based substitution methods to replace sensitive information with generic placeh olders, ensuring that patient identities cannot be inferred from the data. The detailed process is illustrated in Figure 1. Figure 1 HepatoAudit Dataset Construction Flowchart We used GPT-4o to extract the names of diseases involved in preliminary diagnosis and differential diagnosis from patients medical records as options. We selected 20 common hepatobiliary cases, totaling 684 records. Figure 2 shows the distribution of each disease. Finally, we invited professional physicians to manually verify and correct all the data to further optimize data quality. Our goal is to enable large language models to generate clinical diagnoses based on patients basic information, chief comp laints, history of present illness, past medical history, laboratory test results, and

given case options. These diagnoses include key information such as preliminary diagnosis, diagnostic basis, and differential diagnosis. We will compare the disease classification in preliminary and differential diagnoses to assess the performance of current large language models in terms of generating consistency in medical record documentation.The details of the dataset can be found in Table 1 and the examples in the appendix. EVALUATION METHOD We used precision, recall as evaluation metrics. To comprehensively assess the performance of large language models (LLMs) in generating consistent diagnoses within the HepatoAudit dataset, we evaluated six commonly used LLMs, as shown in Table 2. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted using four different prompting methods zero -shot prompting, few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought10, and few- shot chain -of-thought. In the few -shot evaluation, to avoid misleading the LLMs with incorrect examples, we carefully prepared an additional set of 20 diagnostic examples for few - shot prompting and few-shot chain-of-thought. These 20 cases were w ritten by the same physician to ensure stylistic consistency. We extracted key patient information, such as physical indicators, clinical symptoms, and laboratory test results, and used the BM25 algorithm for similarity matching to select the three most similar samples. IV. EXPERIMENT AND ANALYSIS A. Experimental Setup We evaluated large language models on the HepatoAudit dataset using four prompting methods zero -shot, few -shot, chain-of-thought (CoT), and few-shot combined with chain-of- thought (few -shotCoT). We used the

DeepSeekV2 API to extract options from the model outputs and manually reviewed 50 extraction results, achieving 100 accuracy. In the chain-of- thought (CoT) setup, we added the prompt Please analyze the patients condition step -by-step to encourage the models stepwise reasoning ability.The results f or zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and few -shotchain-of-thought settings are reported separately. B. Evaluation Results To comprehensively assess the diagnostic capability of large language models in the HepatoAudit task, we evaluated six commonly used large language models, including three proprietary models and three open -source models known for strong performance across various benchmarks. The results are presented in Table 3 and Table 4. Additionally, we assessed the models accuracy in diagnosing hepatobiliary diseases of different etiologies, with results displayed in Figure 3. Even for GPT -4, which achieves the highest precision across four different prompt methods, the overall recall rate remains below 70. High precision means that the model s predictions for positive cases are mostly correct, which is crucial in medical diagnosis, as incorrect diagnoses can lead to unnecessary treatments and potential harm to patients. However, low recall indicates that the model s overall classification capability is insufficient, failing to identify all true positive cases. This issue

is clearly reflected in Figure 3. For diseases with non -specific symptoms such as splenomegaly and fatty liver, the accuracy of each model is generally below 40, while for multi -factor diseases such as hypertension and type 2 diabetes, the accuracy is typically below 30. These significant shortcomings severely limit the clinical applicability of the models, as the inability to accurately and compre hensively identify a patients condition may lead to delayed treatment or incorrect medical decisions. 237 Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. A manual review of 50 error cases showed that 72 of the errors were due to the large language mode ls inability to fully understand the patient s complete medical history and symptom presentation, leading to premature conclusions and inaccurate diagnoses. Even with the addition of CoT, this problem was not alleviated. Although CoT helps to promote prob lem analysis, the lack of reflection and error correction mechanisms means that initial misunderstandings are amplified

during the reasoning process, affecting the final diagnostic results. V. CONCLUSION In this study, we propose and construct the first dataset specifically designed to evaluate the consistency of large language models (LLMs) in generating diagnostic records for hepatobiliary casesHepatoAudit. This dataset originates from real-world medical records. HepatoAudit covers 20 common hepatobiliary diseases, with a total of 684 test cases. We evaluated the performance of six commonly used LLMs under different prompting methods, finding that while all models achieved high precision, their accuracy and recall rates were relatively low. The LLMs exhibited limitations in understanding patients comprehensive medical histories and symptoms, as well as in identifying underlying causes, especially when dealing with complex records and atypical symptoms, often leading to misdiagnoses or missed diagnoses. Adding chain -of-thought (CoT) prom pting or using few -shot methods did not significantly improve performance, as the models were unable to fully comprehend medical histories and symptoms and lacked effective reflection and error correction mechanisms. This experiment further highlights the challenges of applying LLMs in the medical domain. HepatoAudit will facilitate in -depth analysis of the strengths and weaknesses of LLMs on the HepatoAudit task, helping researchers identify areas for improvement and drive the iterative optimization of LLM s in medical diagnostics. Hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) G. Pradeep Reddy , Y. V. Pavan Kumar , K. Purna Prakash Kookmin University, 77 Jeongneung-ro, Sungbuk-Gu, Seoul 02703, REPUBLIC OF KOREA School of Electronics Engineering, VIT-AP University, Amaravati 522237, Andhra Pradesh, INDIA Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Andhra Pradesh, INDIA gpradeepreddykookmin.ac.kr, pavankumar.yvvitap.ac.in, kpurnaprakashkluniversity.in Abstract The recent advancements in neural network architectures, particularly transformers, have played a cr ucial role in the rapid progress of Large Language Models (LLMs ). LLMs are trained on many parameters. By training these parameters on vast amounts of text data, LLMs can learn t o generate reactions to a wide variety of prompts. These models have enabled machines to generate new data (human-like), driving significant developments in Natural Language Processing (NLP). They have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in producing new content. Besides their impressive per formance, LLMs occasionally generate hallucinatory responses that produce nonsensical or inaccurate information. In sim ple terms, hallucinations in LLMs happen when the model generates information that may sound believable but is actually wr ong. It can make up details or go beyond what it has learned from the training data, resulting in inaccurate output. These hallucinatory responses appear to be authentic but lack grounding in reality. Such hallucinations can include fabrications such as facts, events, or statements that lack support from real-world data. Addressing this issue is important to enhance the reliability of AI-generated content. Hallucinations can be a significant challenge in critical applications such as healthcare, law, etc. In this vi ew, this paper delves into the phenomenon of hallucinations in the context of LLMs. The objective is to understand the causes, explo re the implications, and discuss potential strategies for mitigation. Keywords hallucinations, large language models (LLMs), large language model operations (LLMOps), nonsensical, natural language processing

(NLP), transformers. I. INTRODUCTION Artificial intelligence (AI) has become a ubiquitous technology, with applications in a wide range of industries and fields 1, 2. From speech and image recognition to autonomous vehicles, recommendation systems, smart homes, and virtual assistants, AI is transforming the way peopl e live and work 3-5. Current research in AI is concentrated mo re on developing new methods for making AI systems more explainable and fair. For example, XAI (Explainable AI) is a field of research that strives to develop methods to make AI systems more explainable 6. Besides, the Internet of T hings (IoT), is a network consisting of physical objects tha t are embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity to col lect and exchange data 7, 8. IoT is playing a key role in the advancement of AI by providing a vast amount of data that can be used to train and improve AI models. Large Language Models (LLMs) are a kind of AI that are trained on huge datasets 9, 10. LLMs are advanced NLP (Natural Language Processing) models that use a deep neural network architecture designed to understand, generate, and process human language at a sophisticated level 11. The earliest example of language model invention was ELIZA in 1967, developed by Prof. J. Weizenbaum at the artificial intelligence laboratory of MIT. A breakthrough came in 2017 with the transformer architecture, which made significant advancements in this research 12. Furthermore, with the accessibility of computational power and large amounts of data, LLMs are performing well 13. Since their inception, LLMs have grown with the advancements in machine learning techniques. Researchers are continuously exploring new architectures to further enhance their capabilities. The major developments in the growth of L LMs are shown in Fig. 1. Large Language Model Operations (LLMOps) focus on the challenges

and best practices of deploying and managing LLMs in production environments. Key steps involved in the LLMOps are shown in Fig. 2. The data used to train LLMs is massive and collected from various internet sources, including books, articles, website s, etc. 14. The simplified representation of the entire process is shown in Fig. 3. LLMs can be used in a large range of industries and use cases including retail, healthcare, t ech, etc. 15. The process of fine-tuning an LLM. II. TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE After the invention of the transformer architecture 12, it has evolved as the current advancement in the NLP 16. Researchers are actively exploring the application of transformer architecture in diverse domains (e.g.

vision transformers) 17. Fig. 4 illustrates the high-level view of the transformer architecture. It includes two major parts the decoder and the encoder. The encoder takes the input sequence and processes it to create a sequence of representatio ns. The decoder takes these representations from the encoder and generates an output sequence. Encoder Decoder Nx Nx Input Output Fig. 4. Transformer encoder and decoder architecture. These encoder and decoder layers are not used just once but rather stacked on top of each other, creating a deeper neural network. Each layer refines the representation of th e input or output based on the previous layers. NX in Fig. 4 sig nifies that the transformer architecture utilizes N repetitions of the encoder-decoder structure. The first step in preparing text for analysis is to toke nize the input sequence. The next step is input embedding, which converts the tokens into vectors. Positional encoding helps to capture the position of each token in the input sequence. T he positional encoding precedes the input embedding prior to that they are fed into the encoder. Figure 5 provides a visua l representation of all the steps involved in generating the positional encoding layer output. The output of the posi tional encoding layer is given as input to the encoder blocks parallelly. This is one of the advantages of the transformer network when compared to other neural networks because it can make use of current Graphics processing unit (GPU) parallel processing. This allows the GPU to process m ultiple input sequences at the same time. Whereas in many other conventional neural networks, the input is given sequentially, which means that the GPU can only process one input sequence at a time. This can significa ntly slow down the training process, especially for large input sequences. Thus, parallel

processing enables the transformer s computationally efficient on modern GPU hardware. Each encoder block has one feed-forward layer and one multi-hea d attention layer. Self-attention is a mechanism that allows each word in the input sequence to attend to all other words in the same sequence. It captures the significance of each word in the context of the whole sequence. Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on July 14,2025 at 170957 UTC from IEEE Xplore. The process involves comparing the similarity between each words representation with all other words representations to calculate attention scores (with the help of key (K), que ry (Q), and value (V)). Here, the mathematical operation dot product is used to compute the similarity between two vectors. Self-attention matrix is computed using (1) 12, where, dk refers to the keys dimension. ( )Attention , , softmax T k K Q K Q V V d æ ö ç ç è ø (1) The add norm operation is applied after each sub-layer in the transformer stack, including attention and feed-forward layers. The addition operation is used to add the output o f the multi-head attention mechanism to the input sequence. Lay er normalization is used to normalize the output of the addit ion operation. This process helps in creating a stable and effective model by reducing the vanishing gradient problem during training and improving the flow of information through the network. The feed-forward layer is a fully connected neural network that is given

to each position separately and concurrently. It consists of two linear transformations with a ReLU activation in between. The feed-forward layer allows the model to capture complex patterns and dependencies between words in the input sequence, making it more powerful in handling various NLP tasks. The output of the encoder block is given as input to the decoder block. The decoder block consists of attention, feed-forward, and add norm layers. Here one of the attention layers used is the masked multi-head atte ntion layer. It is used to stop the decoder from attending to futur e words in the output sequence during training. The output of the decoder block is given to linear and softmax layers. Thes e layers are responsible for generating the final predicti ons for the output sequence. III. C AUSES OF HALLUCINATIONS Sometimes LLMs can generate hallucinatory responses that are incorrect and misleading 18. As the outputs are produced in natural language, they may appear very convincing, even though they are wrong. Intrinsic and extrinsic hallucination s are two types of hallucinations 19. Intrinsic hallucination s refer to hallucinations that are generated by manipulating the content in the input. These are like the direct contradict ion between the input and the generated content. For example, if the input to LLM is Ramu likes to play cricket, then the output will be Ramu likes to play basketball. Wherea s extrinsic hallucinations occur when the outputs cannot be validated as true or false based only on the input. For example, if the input to LLM is The weather in Hyderabad is ve ry good, then the output will be The weather in Hyderabad is very good, and it has attracted 2 lakh tourists because of its beautiful weather conditions. It may or may not be true that it

has attracted 2 lakh tourists, but it cant be verified fr om the input. This section discusses some of the major causes of hallucinations. Biases in Training Data LLMs are typically trained on a large corpus of data, which might have imbalances or unfair representations of specific groups or topics, result ing in unintended consequences for the models behavior and outputs. Some features might be linked to class labels falsely which leads models to learn misleading patterns and produce inaccurate predictions. Limited Context LLMs do not understand the context the same as humans do. This leads to hallucinations where the model generates responses that do not closely match the input or fail to provide relevant information. This means sometimes they can produce a response that does not apply to the context. Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Downloaded on July 14,2025 at 170957 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. Ambiguous Prompts An ambiguous prompt means it has several possible meanings or interpretations. When LLMs come across such prompts they depend on their prior knowledge or expectations to fill in gaps leading to hallucinations. If the prompt lacks clarity, the model generates unintended responses that are not as per the user. Adversarial Attacks Adversarial attacks are used to manipulate the input data given to the model to generate misleading or incorrect responses. These attacks focus on the context to produce inaccurate responses. These attacks take advantage of the vulnerabilities in the models behavior, revealing weaknesses and spotlighting areas where the models understanding and reasoning capabilitie s are lacking. Overfitting This occurs when the model learns the training data too well and is incapable of generalizing the new data. This can lead to the model making predictions that are accurate for the training data but are inaccurate for new data.

Its essential to recognize the potential for ove rfitting to result in hallucinations in LLMs. When using LLMs, critical evaluation of the output is crucial, as its i mportant to be aware of the possibility that the LLM is generat ing a response that is inaccurate or nonsensical. Model Architecture The model architecture includes the design and structure, such as the parameters or number of layers. Increased complexity in models with more layers or parameters can make them more prone to the generation of hallucinations. The architecture, complexity, covering design, and learning mechanisms, ominously influence behavior and proneness to produce hallucinatory responses. IV. I MPLICATIONS OF HALLUCINATIONS The effects of hallucinations can be based on the frequency of occurrence, specific type, and underlying cause behind them 20. This section discusses the general implications of t he hallucinations as shown in Fig. 6. A. Ethical Considerations 1) Spread of misinformation When LLMs produce fabricated or incorrect information, people may conside r this and share these details which results in web deceptive content. Bad actors take advantage of this vulnerability to impact public opinion or promote untruths, causing a serious imp act on public discourse. 2) Legal and regulatory challenges Generating false or misleading information by an LLM can lead to legal liab ility. In addition to that, there are numerous points to look at such as liability for harm caused by false information and intellectual property rights. Accountability and bal ancing innovation are very important to address the legal implications of hallucinations. B. Social Impact 1) Erosion of trust When LLMs produce misleading information, it can destroy peoples trust in the inform ation they receive. This leads to a lot of problems. Trust pl ays an important role in the wide acceptance of LLMs. If users perceive that LLM is inclined

towards hallucinations, it will raise doubts about the reliability and dependability of it s outputs. Such erosion of trust can obstruct the adoption of LLMs. 2) Influence on decision-making Hallucinations can influence peoples beliefs, which can then affect thei r decision-making. For example, if an LLM generates a response that supports a particular belief, it can make people more likely to believe that belief. This can lead to peo ple making decisions that are consistent with that belief, even if the belief is not true. C. Practical Consequences 1) Damaging reputation and brand image Hallucinations can significantly harm the credibility and reputation o f the companies or organizations that use LLMs. Inaccurate or misleading outputs from LLMs not only erode trust in the technology itself but also raise doubts about the compet ence and reliability of the entities implementing them. 2) Risks in critical applications In applications where accuracy is very important, such as medical diagnoses, legal analysis, or autonomous systems, the risks associated w ith hallucinations are particularly severe. Hallucinatory information in these complex domains leads to improper diagnoses, legal issues, accidents, or other terrible consequences. V. M ITIGATION STRATEGIES LLMs are moving into the deployment phase at scale and are used by a huge number of users. So, its important to know various strategies which help to minimize the effect of hallucinations in LLMs. This section talks about some of th e important mitigation strategies. Dataset Curation Dataset curation is the process of selecting, cleaning, and organizing data for a certain objective. It is important to address biases and make sure the training data is balanced and representative. In the context of LLMs, dataset curation helps to reduce hallucinations by making sure the data used to train the model is reliable, relevant, and accurate. Improving Context Representation Adding

some more context, such as historical information or user-specific data can help the model to produce responses that are more contextually relevant and aligned with the users intent. Prompt engineering can help refine input prompts. It provides clear instructions to the model. Users can guide the model by carefully crafting prompts that lead to generating more accurate and non-hallucinating responses. Human-in-the-loop Approaches These approaches involve human feedback or interference in the training, testing, or deployment of LLMs 21. Humans evaluate and validate the accuracy of the models responses, which helps in reducing the probability of hallucinatory responses. Authorized licensed use limited to University of Koblenz. Potential implications of hallucinations. Filtering Mechanisms Filtering mechanisms are used for reducing hallucinations with LLMs both in terms of frequency and severity. By validating the generated outputs, these methods detect and mitigate hallucinations, thereby it flags or correct outputs that are not accurate. Factua l verification and logic filters are two examples of filters that are helpful. Regularization Techniques Regularization techniques are methods that prevent overfitting or memorization of training data by LLMs. Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data very closely, and as an outcome, it is unable to generalize the new data. Techniques such as dropout encourage the model to generalize rather than memorize the training data. This reduces the risk of the model generating erroneous output based on memorized information. VI. S UMMARY The phenomenon of hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) poses a substantial challenge that needs to be solved to ensure the

reliability and accuracy of AI-gen erated content. While LLMs have proven remarkable capabili ties in generating good responses, the occasional generation of nonsensical or inaccurate information can undermine their trustworthiness. This paper explored the causes and implications of these hallucinations in LLMs. Further, various mitigation strategies are also discussed. The collabora tion between researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders is important to ensure that the developments align with soc ietal values. With responsible deployment, LLMs can become powerful tools for advancing human progress across a wide range of applications. R EFERENCES 1 G. P. Reddy and Y. V. 2024 International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM) 979-8-3503-8622-62431.00 2024 IEEE 6474 MedGen An Explainable Multi-Agent Architecture for Clinical Decision Support through Multisource Knowledge Fusion Ziji Liu School of Computer Science Hubei University of Technology Wuhan, China 0009-0004-6256-6293 Liang Xiao School of Computer Science Hubei University of Technology Wuhan, China 0000-0002-1564-2466 Rujun Zhu School of Computer Science Hubei University of Technology Wuhan, China 0009-0005-9014-1783 Hang Yang School of Computer Science Hubei University of Technology Wuhan, China 0009-0002-0302-8052 Miaomiao He School of Computer Science Hubei University of Technology Wuhan, China 0009-0008-3166-4711 AbstractAgents in medical decision support have been extensively researched, particularly in areas like evidence support, multimorbidity management, and patient-spe cific needs. However, current approaches lack a unified m ethod to address real-time evidence updates, manage multiple diseases independently, and incorporate personalized patient needs. Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents are limited b y their reliance on static knowledge bases, hindering their ability to promptly update clinical guidelines and meet div erse patient requirements. Moreover, the interpretability of LLMs remains a significant concern, leading to skepticism in the ir medical application. To address these challenges, we developed MedGen, a multi-agent architecture that decomposes the clinical decision- making process into stages such as clinical goal se tting, data collection, argumentation linking, and plan selecti on. This structured approach allows LLM agents to provide both reasoning evidence and a transparent reasoning proc ess, enhancing the reliability and interpretability of o utcomes. Finally, a case study of breast cancer and depressi on is combined to illustrate our architecture. Keywordsclinical decision support, multi-agent syst ems, evidence based medicine, large language model I. INTRODUCTION Clinical decision support, particularly when addressing the complexities of multimorbidity management, is a hig hly intricate process. Multi-agent architectures for cl inical decision-making are developed to assist in this dom ain by

integrating agents with various functions to tackle complex clinical decisions 1. Existing clinical decision support systems have conducted independent studies on drug interaction management in multimorbidity 2, evide nce- based argumentation 3, and personalized care 4. However, when addressing these issues individually, traditio nal multi- agent systems face several limitations that urgentl y require resolution Due to the lack of mechanisms for interaction with external knowledge sources, the knowledge base in a gent frameworks is fixed at the time of development and remains closed. Although some designs allow for updates to the knowledge base, these updates are often delayed. In the context of multimorbidity management, certain metho ds simplify the problem to drug interactions without considering the overall conflict between treatments for differe nt diseases 2. This approach may exclude drug combinations th at have weaker interactions but provide better overall ther apeutic outcomes. In clinical decision-making, Electronic H ealth Records (EHR) are effectively used to customize cli nical decision recommendations for patients 5. However, patient- specific needs are rarely considered, and the conflict between treatment efficacy and the patients quality of lif e during treatment has not been adequately addressed in prev ious methods. While there are methods in individual domains that address some of these issues, there is currently no unified approach that comprehensively considers these clini cal decision problems and provides integrated treatment recommendations. Based on these considerations, we have designed a multi-agent interactive architecture based on LLM- Agent. Through the collaboration of various agents, our architecture collects data from PubMed, NICE, and p atient discussion platforms. We employ an improved PICOAS (Patient, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome, Argume nt, Sentiment) modeling method, based on the traditiona l PICO elements, to convert unstructured statements from c linical guidelines, medical literature, and patient reviews into structured elements suitable for argumentation.

The PICOAS elements from different sources are then linked acc ording to their argumentation relationships to form an argume ntation graph centered on the clinical guideline elements. We have organized the clinical decision-making proc ess into several steps setting clinical goals, data co llection, PICOAS extraction, argumentation linking, summarizi ng arguments, and evaluating the decision plan. Each of these steps is executed by different types of agents, whi ch collaborate through predefined interaction methods. Although the closed nature of LLMs makes it challenging to understand their specific problem-solving mechanisms, in our architecture, the data processed by each agent at every step is derived from transparent information passed on from the previous step, with sources rooted in literature. This approach minimizes the instances where the LLM supplements arguments with its own medical knowledge, thereby enhancing the reliability of the generated results and ensuring that these results are traceable. It addresses challenges such as the lack of real-time evidence, difficulties in managing multip le diseases, and unmet patient-specific needs in clini cal decision-making. Development of an interpretable LLM-Agent architecture for clinical decision-making This architecture leverages the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to focus on the extraction and linking of data, ensuring that the knowledge generated at each step is based on evidence from existing literature.

The inclusion of an Argument Summarizer further enhance s the systems interpretability and reliability by summarizing the linking and reasoning processes. II. R ELATED WORK A. Agent-Assisted Clinical Decision Support Agent-assisted Clinical Decision Support (CDS) syst ems leverage intelligent agent technology to help healt hcare professionals make more accurate and effective deci sions during diagnosis and treatment. For example, iHANDs analyzes electronic health records (EHRs) and uses knowledge from databases to guide clinical decision s 5. Similarly, MET4 facilitates the collaboration of interdisciplinary healthcare teams (IHTs) through multi-agent systems 6. However, existing systems still have l imitations in managing the clinical care of multiple diseases and addressing patient-specific needs. Additionally, they struggle to achieve real-time updates of arguments within the decision- making process. B. LLM-Empowered Agent Architecture The LLM-Agents architecture integrates large langua ge models (LLMs) with a multi-agent system to tackle c omplex clinical decision-making problems. In this architec ture, objectives are broken down into executable tasks, with agents collaborating to achieve the final goal 7. Some LLM-agents are endowed with the capability to interact with ex ternal knowledge sources, overcoming the limitations of traditional agent systems 8. MetaGPT 9 introduces roles fro m different engineering positions within the software development process into the LLM-Agent architecture , creating an automated software development team. Th is concept can similarly be applied to the clinical de cision- making domain. C. PICO, PROforma, and Patient Sentiment Models The PICO (Patient, Intervention, Comparison, Outcom e) model is a widely used architecture in clinical res earch, designed to structure the formulation and answering of clinical questions 10. The PICO model facilitates systemat ic retrieval and evaluation of clinical evidence, ther eby enhancing the quality and reliability of research. PROforma is a modeling language used for clinical pathways and decision support 11, capable of describing

and executing c linical decision processes, supporting complex treatment pl ans and multimorbidity management. The patient sentiment mo del 12, by analyzing patient emotions and feedback, a ssists clinicians in better understanding patient needs an d preferences, thereby improving the effectiveness of personalized treatment. These modeling approaches h ave been extensively studied within their respective do mains however, there is a lack of a method that can effec tively integrate them into a unified framework. III. A RCHITECTURE OVERVIEW The MedGen system is a multi-agent conversational architecture designed to provide multisource knowle dge fusion and explainable clinical decision support. E ach agent within the system utilizes the GPT-4 model for natu ral language processing and context understanding to ge nerate coherent and contextually appropriate responses. Ho wever, their interaction is strictly regulated instead of functioning as a hospital composed of various specialists, the agents operate as a data collection and organization team centered around a particular expert. This configuration provides real -time, multimorbidity management, and personalized care fo r clinical decision support. To facilitate transparent data integration from dif ferent sources, we developed the PICOAS method, which builds on the medical research elements of PICO. This method elementizes content from clinical guidelines, medic al literature, and patient reviews to enable linkage a nd argumentation through these elements. The elementiz ation methods for different sources of literature are as follows Clinical Guidelines For a specific disease, clinical guidelines typically associate symptoms with corresponding treatment measures. Therefore, the primary elements extracted are P (Patient) and I (Intervention). Medical Literature Most medical research papers follow the PICO format, allowing us to directly extract PI CO elements from the abstracts of these studies. This provides the P(Patient), I (Intervention), C (Comparison), and O (Outcome) studied in the literature. Patient Reviews Patient reviews primarily convey

subjective experiences. In our previous study 6, we modeled these experiences as sentiments. In this pa per, we build on that work by modeling patient reviews using the P (Patient), I (Intervention), C (Comparison), and S (Sentiment) architecture, where C (Comparison) is n ot always present. Argumentation Integration When the O (Outcome) from medical literature and S (Sentiment) from pati ent reviews are integrated into the argumentation cente red around clinical guidelines, they are converted into supporting or opposing Arguments (A) for their respective I (Intervention) The linking patterns designed to address the three issues are illustrated in Fig. 1. Three categories of knowledge linking patterns Legend CG Clinical Guideline CD Clinical Decision P Patient, Population, or Problem I1, I2 Intervention 1, Intervention 2 C Comparison O Outcome S Sentiment A Argument ML Medical Literature PR Patient Review The MedGen architecture processes data from three different sources through the collaborative efforts of multiple agents, linking them based on PICOAS elements to fo rm an argumentation graph. In this process, the agents do not generate new knowledge instead, they extract and i ntegrate knowledge from the literature. The architecture the n makes judgments based on these argumentations, ensuring t hat the evidence provided for clinical decision support is derived from literature sources rather than being generated by ChatGPT itself. This approach enhances the reliabil ity and transparency of the clinical decision-making process. IV. AGENT TYPES AND THEIR ROLE SPECIFICATIONS MedGen achieves its final objectives through the integration and collaboration of agents with distinct functions. Each agent is constrained to perform only the tasks within its designated responsibilities and is equipped with a reflective function. For agents focused on

data collection and linkage, such as the Knowledge Manager, their ability to exp and or supplement information using the LLMs inherent knowledge is restricted. These agents are limited to extracting and linking data from different sources strictly within the bou nds of predefined prompts. Similarly, agents with reasonin g capabilities, such as the Decision Generator, are a lso subject to constraints. The decisions they generate must be based solely on the data collected by other agents. In the following sections, we will delve into the roles and responsibilities of each agent within the architect ure by exploring a specific clinical decision-making case. This will illustrate how each agent contributes to the overal l decision- making process within MedGen. TABLE I. provides an overview of these agents. TABLE I. AGENT ROLES AND OUTPUTS IN MED GEN ARCHITECTURE Agent Roles and output Goal planner Decision goals Knowledge manager PICOAS knowledge pieces Paern selector Linking modes Decision generator Recommenda ons generated using linked knowledge model Argument summarizer Summary and review Outcome veriﬁer Evaluated results against cri cal ques ons (CQs) A. Goal Planner The Goal Planners task is to identify the patient s symptoms or diagnosed conditions within the case an d to establish a set of clinically addressable issues ba sed on the appropriate positive treatment approaches for these conditions and symptoms. The Goal Planner collects and interpr ets information from the EHR and clinical decision-making data related to the patient, then determines the treatment objectives. It breaks down the overall treatment goal into mult iple independent, manageable targets. These goals are not immutable the Goal Planners r ole is to propose the initial objectives, which may be adj usted later in the process based on clinical guidelines. The Goal Planner provides foundational support for the clinical reas oning by ensuring that

the goals are accurately set accordin g to established guidelines. These objectives then serve as benchmarks for the final evaluation, helping to verify whether the outcomes meet the established goals and ensuring that the final decisions directly address the identified iss ues, rather than deviating from the patients needs. B. Pattern selector In our previous work, we designed three PICOAS-base d linking models to address three distinct clinical problems. The Pattern Selector is responsible for determining which linking modality is required to solve the identified issues. These three different modalities correspond to addressing the c hallenges of up-to-date evidence integration, multimorbidity management, and personalized care. Solving these pr oblems necessitates accessing and processing the latest cl inical guidelines, medical literature, and patient reviews, generating corresponding Knowledge Managers, assigning them th e target data they need to collect and process, and m onitoring these concurrent Knowledge Managers. The activation of these linking modalities is based on the patients condition and revolves around clinical guidelines to address clin ical decision-making challenges Supplementing Up-to-date Evidence This modality focuses on enriching clinical guidelines with evidence drawn from medical literature that may not yet be incorporated into the guidelines. These findings could support, oppose, or introduce new therapies into the clinical guidelines. Therefore, the literature search prioritizes recent studies from the past few years, or even months, to ensure the integration of the most current evidence. Multimorbidity Management When a patients condition involves the management of multiple diseases simultaneously, clinical guidelines may only focus on the specific disease they represent, potentially overlooking the complexities of multimorbidity management. In this modality, the literature search is concentrated on studies that mention two or more diseases or symptoms together, using clinical literature to establish connections between otherwise independent clinical guidelines. Personalized care When a patients personal preferences are

incorporated, this modality emphasizes searching for ways to alleviate the patients discomfort or meet their personal wishes. Knowledge manager The Knowledge Manager is activated upon receiving a task from the Pattern Selector. Each Knowledge Mana ger, depending on the assigned task, processes data from specified sources according to predefined rules. A single Kno wledge Manager is responsible only for the retrieval and extraction of data from its designated source. The specific steps are as follows Literature Retrieval The Knowledge Manager searches relevant literature databases based on the task and keywords it received. It uses tools like w eb crawlers according to predefined rules to retrieve literature and filters out those that meet the task requirements. For medical literature, the abstracts are primarily retrieved from PubMed, while clinical guidelines are searched within NICEs database. The prioritization of extraction, such as journal name and impact factor restrictions, can be customized as needed. Patient reviews are sourced from open patient discussion platforms, with appropriate anonymization and privacy protection measures applied. PICOAS Element Extraction The extraction process varies depending on the data source, with different rules applied to process the elements. PICOAS ELEMENTS ACROSS DIFFERENT DATA SOURCES Data source PICOAS elements Clinical guideline P (Pa ent, Popula on, or Problem) I (Interven on) Medical literature P (Pa ent, Popula on, or Problem) I (Interven on) C (Comparison) O (Outcome) Pa ent review P (Pa

ent, Popula on, or Problem) I (Interven on) C (Comparison) S (Sen ment) Linking PICOAS Elements After extracting the PICOAS elements, the Knowledge Manager links these elements by using the P (Patient) and I (Intervention) elements from the clinical guidelines as the starting point. It compares these elements with the P elements from medical literature and patient reviews, associating the data relevant to the patie nts condition and the clinical guidelines. This process results in the formation of an argumentation graph that centers around the clinical guidelines. During the processing of PICOAS elements and the generation of the argumentation, the LLM is restric ted from generating any information that is not sourced from the original data. This ensures that the content within the PICOAS elements is exclusively derived from the original t exts of clinical guidelines, medical literature, and patien t reviews. This restriction maintains the integrity and traceability of the information used in the clinical decision-making process. D. Decision generator Once the argumentation graph is generated, all supporting and opposing arguments for different treatment opti ons are clearly presented. The Decision Generator analyzes each argumentation chain within the graph, aiming to ful fill as many of the objectives set by the Goal Planner as p ossible. The Decision Generator identifies and selects the most robust and promising argumentation paths as potential solutions. The Decision Generator will describe the argument graph in natural language. Prior to this, the Knowledge Manager will provide the argument graph to the Decision Generato r in a data format rather than as an image. Fig. 2. Conversion of Argumentation Graph into Natural Language Reasoning by the Decision Generator To ensure objectivity in the decision-making proces s, multiple Decision Generators are employed. Each Dec ision Generator independently interprets the argumentatio n graph and makes its final

judgment. These independent decisions are then summarized by the Argument Summarizer. The Argument Summarizer compiles the reasoning and conclusions from each Decision Generator and organi zes a voting process to select the final decision. This c ollaborative approach helps ensure that the chosen solution is t he most well-rounded and aligned with the goals initially s et, while also being supported by transparent and well-docume nted reasoning. E. Argument Summarizer To minimize data loss and misunderstandings during agent interactions, all key communications are standardiz ed for precise data transmission. The architecture operate s with complete transparency during problem-solving, ensuring that the reasoning process is fully visible. When the Decision Generator reaches a decision, the Argument Summarizer reviews the entire process of d ata collection, linkage, and reasoning. It presents the results with detailed explanations, providing a step-by-step sum mary of the reasoning process. This transparency allows res earchers and the Outcome Verifier to assess the overall reliability of the results. The Argument Summarizer is also responsibl e for addressing any specific questions or concerns raise d by researchers or outcome validators regarding the rea soning process. F. Outcome verifier The Outcome Verifier interacts with the Argument Summarizer and poses questions about several critical aspects of the reasoning process Achievement of Goals Set by the Goal Planner The Outcome Verifier assesses whether the results successfully meet the objectives established by the Goal Planner, ensuring that the proposed solution i s aligned with the intended clinical goals. Argument Summarizers Process of Summarizing Reasoning and Tracing Outcomes in Response to Outcome Verifiers Queries The Argument Summarizer can trace back through the reasoning process to address questions posed by the Outcome Verifier, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The transparency and thoroughness of the argumentation provide the foundation for evaluating the reliability and validity of the proposed clinical decision. V. A GENT INTERACTION PROTOCOLS FOR ADDRESSING THREE HIGH -LEVEL DECISION GOALS In handling different types of problems, the relati onship between agents can be understood as a pipeline process. After one agent completes its task, it passes the results to the next agent for further processing. Of course, there may be instances where agents interact directly with each other to a ddress specific issues. For example, the Outcome Verifier might engage in a dialogue with the Argument Summarizer to clarify any doubts arising from the summarized content. MedGens approach to resolving challenges related to real- time evidence integration, multimorbidity managemen t, and patient feedback involves specific roles and intera ctions among agents, as illustrated in Fig. 4. This workflow ensures that each agent focuses on it s designated tasks, contributing to a streamlined and efficient decision-making process while maintaining the abili ty to address queries or concerns that arise during the p ipeline process. The systems structured and transparent design allows for efficient handling of complex clinical scenarios, ensuring that each aspect of the problem is thoroughly analy zed and

addressed. Fig. 4. Workflow of Multi-Agent Collaboration in MedGen for Solving Clinical Decision-Making Problems A. An agent interaction protocol for keeping guidelines up- to-date In MedGens approach, clinical guidelines, which ar e periodically updated based on the latest medical research and clinical practices, may not reflect the most recent findings between update cycles. To maintain the real-time relevance of clinical guidelines, the Knowledge Manager retrieves articles from authoritative medical journals published in recent years, focusing on those related to clinical decision-making. Most of these articles can be accessed via open-source plat forms like PubMed. The Knowledge Manager extracts elements from the collected abstracts according to PICO rules, identi fying the patient population, experimental group, control gro up, and conclusions of the studies. These elements, along with the PI elements extracted from the clinical guidelines, ar e then submitted to the Decision Generator for linking and subsequent judgment. The Decision Generator compares the P elements to determine whether the patient population described in the clinical guidelines and the medical literature corresponds to or includes the current patient. If there is a match, the information is included in the linking process if not, it is e xcluded. The Decision Generator then classifies the literature b ased on the relationship between I (Intervention) and C (Comparison) and the conclusions (O) as either supporting the curren t clinical decision, opposing it, or suggesting a new clinical decision option. Once successfully linked, the O element fro m the literature is transformed into an A (Argument) elem ent that supports or opposes the decision within the clinical guidelines. An agent interaction protocol for multi-morbidity Multimorbidity management has always been a particularly complex issue in clinical decision-mak ing, as clinical guidelines typically focus on the treatment of a single disease, often overlooking the complexities of mana ging multiple conditions simultaneously. In the MedGen approach, the preliminary steps for handling multimorbidity are the same as those previously described. However, the search focus shifts to medical literature that discusses co-occurring diseases, with a broader time frame allowed for the search. When these PICO elements are submitted to the Decision Generator, the generator matches the multiple diseases of the patient with

the P (Patient) elements in the litera ture. If the literatures P element includes all of the diseases that the patient is suffering from, the medical literature is included in the linking sequence. The literature is then catego rized into three types supporting the current clinical decision, opposing the current clinical decision, or suggesting a new clinical decision option. The third column of TABLE III. outlines the triggering conditions for these classifications. This method ensures that the complexity of managing multiple diseases is taken into account, allowing f or a more holistic approach to clinical decision-making that integrates evidence-based practices from multiple sources. Thi s systematic incorporation of relevant literature helps to address the gaps in traditional clinical guidelines when de aling with patients suffering from multiple concurrent conditions. C. An agent interaction protocol for customized care In current clinical decision support systems, addre ssing patient-specific needs is often based more on the p hysicians experience rather than on scientific evidence. In M edGen, medical literature and other patient reviews can se rve as the evidence to support personalized patient needs. The Knowledge Manager abstracts the patients personali zed needs into PICS (where C may not be present) elements, with S representing the patients subjective emotional r esponse to the I (Intervention) treatment plan. By conducting sentiment analysis on the S element, the system can assess the patients subjective feelings toward the proposed treatment plan. Simultaneously, the Knowle dge Manager also searches medical literature for altern atives or solutions that align with the patients needs by re trieving evidence related to other I elements within clinical guidelines. This includes identifying potential substitute interventions or complementary strategies that could better meet the patients personal preferences and circumstances. This approach allows MedGen to incorporate scientif ic evidence into the decision-making process, ensuring

that the patients individual needs are not only acknowledged but also addressed with the support of data-driven arguments . This enhances the quality and personalization of clinica l care, moving beyond reliance on physician experience alon e to a more comprehensive and evidence-based practice. VI. C ASE STUDY We will illustrate the operational workflow of MedG en through a case study involving the management of co morbid breast cancer and depression. This example will dem onstrate the tasks completed by each agent at various stages of the process. Input Case Alice is a 45 -year -old perimenopausal woman (not fully menopausal) with early-stage ER-posi ve breast cancer. She underwent breast-conserving surgery and is currently receiving chemotherapy and taking tamoxifen. Due to the side eﬀects of surgery and chemotherapy, as well as tamoxifen-induced osteoporosis and hot ﬂ ashes, Alice has developed depression and recently complained of insomnia due to hot ﬂashes. Goal Planner Based on the provided case, the follo wing three clinical decision support goals were established Goal 1 Provide endocrine therapy to treat breast cancer. Goal 2 Provide an depressant therapy for Alice. Goal 3 Alleviate Alices hot ﬂashes. Paern Selector Given the case, the following tasks were set to retrieve clinical guidelines, medical literature, and pa ent reviews Task 1 Retrieve and process breast cancer clinical guidelines from NICE. Task 2 Retrieve and process depression clinical guidelines from NICE. Task 3 Search PubMed for literature where depress ion and breast cancer keywords co-occur. Task 4 Search a breast cancer pa ent review datase t for symptoms related to hot ﬂashes. Task 5 Search PubMed for literature on hot ﬂashes related to endocrine therapy for breast cancer. Knowledge Manager Completed the above ﬁve retrieval tasks, extracted relevant data from the literature, and created a li nked argumenta on graph.

C ONCLUSIONS AND DISCUSSION This paper introduces a multi-agent architecture ca lled MedGen, which integrates clinical goals, authoritat ive data, and patient-specific needs to provide comprehensive clinical decision support. First, we outline

the PICOAS elements and the three linking modalities. We then detail the fu nctionality and specific configurations of each agent. Following this, we describe how the agents collaborate to address three different types of problems. Finally, we illustrate the opera tional workflow of our framework through a case study invo lving the combined management of breast cancer and depression. By utilizing a streamlined multi-agent framework, clinical decision support is transformed into a data processing pipeline, where decisions are made based on argument graphs f ormed by linking data from various sources. The systems interpretability is ensured through the summarizati on and retracing of the reasoning process. In summary, thi s paper achieves the following three research objectives Multisource Knowledge Integration By integrating clinical guidelines, medical literature, and patient reviews, MedGen provides real-time, authoritative, and diverse decision support. The use of the PICOAS modeling method allows unstructured clinical data to be transformed into structured elements suitable for argumentation, thereby enhancing the comprehensiveness and reliability of the reasoning process. Transparent Reasoning Process Through the collaboration of multiple agents, each focusing on specific tasks, MedGen achieves transparency and interpretability in its reasoning process. This des ign not only increases the systems credibility but als o ensures that each step of the reasoning process can be traced and verified. Patient-Centered Support MedGen incorporates patient reviews and sentiment analysis to better understand and respond to patient-specific needs. By thoroughly considering these needs, the decisions generated by the system are not only scientifically sound but also better aligned with the actual requirements of the patient. While MedGen has demonstrated significant advantage s in our experiments, future research and practical applications can focus on several areas for further enhancement. These include optimizing agent interactions to reduce red undancy and improve overall system efficiency, expanding th e functionalities

of agents to address more complex c linical decision-making challenges, and incorporating additional data sources like Electronic Health Records (EHRs), genomic data, and real-time monitoring data to enrich the system s knowledge base and enhance decision accuracy and personalization. Additionally, validating MedGen in real clinical settings, developing more user-friendly interfaces, and providing user training and support will improve ac ceptance and satisfaction among healthcare professionals. Fi nally, a strong emphasis on data security and privacy protec tion is essential, with the introduction of advanced securi ty mechanisms to ensure the safety and confidentiality of patient data.
